Unknown.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agronomy, governance, and gender-inclusive adaptation** across Africa and global food systems through evidence-based datasets, policy frameworks, and innovative agricultural technologies. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Evidence for Resilient Agriculture (ERA) initiative** synthesized 2,916 studies to evaluate climate-adaptive agronomic practices in Africa, enabling policy design for soil fertility and intercropping (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/C3YBNN).
- **CIAT and Bioversity International** developed financial feasibility maps for solar-powered irrigation systems to assess cost-efficiency under climate change scenarios in Africa (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/J0Q9TO).
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture, and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** advanced gender-smart agriculture frameworks to integrate adaptive strategies for women in climate-resilient practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/117523).
Unknown.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Evidence for Resilient Agriculture (ERA)** compiled 2,916 studies (112,859 observations) to assess climate-resilient agronomy, soil fertility, and intercropping in Africa, supporting policy and investment decisions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/C3YBNN).
• **CGIAR’s Animal Ontology for Meta-analysis (AOM) v2** standardized livestock feed trial data across 600+ publications, improving resilience in diverse agro-ecological systems through FAIR data principles (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/75E7HV).
• **CGIAR’s Climate Smart Governance Dashboard** integrated nine modules (adaptation catalogs, NAPs, NDC trackers) to enhance national climate action and transformative adaptation planning (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169187).
• **CCAFS** developed gender-responsive Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) strategies, addressing adaptive capacities and empowerment gaps for women in climate-vulnerable regions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/117523).
• **CGIAR’s Climate Services** linked CSA adoption to climate knowledge transfer, supporting resilience-building in agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/117328).
• **IFPRI** reviewed climate impacts on agrifood systems and evaluated policy options for adaptation and mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174179).
• **CGIAR’s Livestock and Climate Initiative** promoted improved forages (drought-tolerant grasses/legumes) to enhance livestock productivity and climate adaptation in LMICs (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173226).
Unknown.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s DSSAT Crop Model** projected yield changes (2005–2050) under SSP370/SSP585 scenarios, analyzing CO₂ fertilization effects and greenhouse gas emissions impacts on seven key crops (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MXJDUC).
• **CGIAR’s Livestock and Climate Initiative** identified improved forages as a mitigation strategy to reduce livestock sector greenhouse gas emissions while increasing drought tolerance (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173226).
• **IFPRI and CGIAR** assessed greenhouse gas emissions reductions and adaptive capacity in food systems as part of climate-smart agriculture frameworks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174179, https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896294257_01).
Unknown.water: • **CIAT and Bioversity International** mapped financial feasibility of solar irrigation systems (vs. diesel) under 21 climate scenarios, optimizing groundwater-based food production in Africa (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/J0Q9TO).

Kenya.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience, policy coherence, and agricultural productivity** in Kenya through **adaptation-focused interventions, policy analyses, and gender-inclusive climate-smart practices**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** conducted gender-disaggregated surveys in Wote and Nyando to assess climate shocks, coping strategies, and adaptation disparities between men and women (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/RBW801, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JU7QP6).
- **CGIAR researchers** evaluated the impact of **Risk Contingent Credit (RCC) with index-based insurance** on food security and resilience in Machakos County, using panel data from 2017–2020 (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OBZUCX).
- **The Center** analyzed **policy coherence** in Kenya’s livestock sector to align adaptation and mitigation strategies with climate and development goals (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101262).
Kenya.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** conducted **gender-disaggregated climate surveys** in Wote and Nyando, Kenya, to identify disparities in climate shock responses and adaptation strategies between men and women (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/RBW801, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JU7QP6).

• **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **Risk Contingent Credit (RCC) with index-based insurance** (triggered by low rainfall) to enhance food security and resilience among smallholder farmers in Machakos County, using a randomized controlled trial (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OBZUCX).

• **CGIAR reviewed Kenya’s climate policies** to assess integration of **adaptation, agricultural productivity, and food security**, highlighting institutional overlaps and the need for stronger coordination (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119747, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110582).

• **CIAT (CGIAR Research Program on Climate-Smart Agriculture)** conducted a **cost-benefit analysis of climate-smart soil practices** in Western Kenya (Bungoma, Kakamega, Siaya) to reduce soil erosion, improve fertility, and stabilize crop/livestock production (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/K6JQXC).

• **CGIAR analyzed climate risks to bean production** in Kenya, documenting declining yields and regional vulnerabilities (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107723).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** assessed **policy coherence for livestock adaptation-mitigation synergies**, identifying gaps in detailed strategies despite Kenya’s emphasis on integration (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101262).
Kenya.mitigation:
• **CGIAR analyzed policy coherence** in Kenya’s livestock sector to evaluate **mitigation strategies and adaptation-mitigation synergies**, finding limited detailed guidance despite alignment with SDGs (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101262).

• **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Villages initiative** documented **success stories of climate-smart agriculture interventions** in East Africa, including Kenya, with potential for scaling (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/81030).
Kenya.water: • **CIAT (CGIAR)** assessed **climate-smart soil practices** in Western Kenya to **reduce soil erosion, improve soil-nitrogen cycles, and enhance water retention** (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/K6JQXC).

Ethiopia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural transformation** in **Ethiopia** through evidence-based interventions and policy frameworks. Specifically:
- **IFPRI** evaluated multisectoral graduation models to strengthen climate resilience, food security, and livelihoods under the **Productive Safety Net Programme (PSNP4)** (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NYTIMZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QDOZPM, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MBRDZ7).
- **Researchers** designed and tested **sustainable land management (SLM) bundles**—including fruit tree planting, composting, and home gardening—to enhance climate adaptation for extremely poor households (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BELDFL).
- **CGIAR** developed a **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) roadmap** to institutionalize adaptation strategies under Ethiopia’s **Climate Resilient Green Economy (CRGE) strategy** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110993).
Ethiopia.adaptation:
• **IFPRI** conducted **clustered randomized controlled trials (RCTs)** to measure the causal impact of **multisectoral graduation packages** (livelihoods, nutrition, gender, climate resilience) on PSNP4 households in Amhara and Oromia regions, covering baseline (2018), midline (2019), and endline (2021) surveys (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NYTIMZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QDOZPM, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MBRDZ7).

• **Researchers** implemented a **sustainable land management (SLM) intervention** (fruit trees, composting, home gardening) via a randomized trial across 95 sub-districts, assessing adoption and impacts among PSNP beneficiaries (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BELDFL).

• **CGIAR** provided a **theory-of-change guide** for monitoring climate adaptation, with case studies from Ethiopia illustrating practical applications in CSA and institutional planning (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/126808).

• **Policy analysis** revealed gaps in integrating **climate adaptation with agricultural productivity** across Eastern Africa, including Ethiopia, and recommended stronger coordination between stakeholders (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119747).

• **CGIAR** documented **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) success stories** from East Africa, highlighting scalable interventions for smallholder farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/81030).

• **Researchers** analyzed the **nexus of climate change, agriculture, and migration**, projecting increased droughts and outmigration risks for Ethiopian youth due to declining agricultural livelihoods (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278).
Ethiopia.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** assessed **policy coherence for livestock sector mitigation and adaptation** in Ethiopia, identifying limited but emerging strategies for reducing GHG emissions while addressing climate risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101262).

• **Researchers** included **climate-smart agriculture interventions** in mitigation frameworks, though detailed mitigation strategies remained underdeveloped compared to adaptation efforts (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/81030).

India.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in India advanced climate-resilient agrifood systems through gender-inclusive assessments, experiential learning tools, and climate-smart interventions. Specifically:
- **TAFSSA** linked farm production, climate risk responses, and natural resource management across household and community levels (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WQBKVH*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/W50RXR*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2YQNRF*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DBAFZY*).
- **CCAFS/CIMMYT** scaled Climate-Smart Villages to enhance resilience and reduce emissions in Haryana and vulnerable states (Sources: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/42346*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/88235*).
India.adaptation:
• **TAFSSA** conducted district-level agrifood systems assessments in Nalanda, integrating climate risk responses, agricultural land use, and livelihood strategies across female, male, adolescent, and community perspectives (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WQBKVH*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/W50RXR*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2YQNRF*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2YQNRF*).

• **CCAFS/CIMMYT** implemented Climate-Smart Villages in Haryana to adopt resilient agricultural practices and reduce vulnerability to extreme climatic events (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/42346*).

• **CCAFS** scaled climate-smart interventions in Uttar Pradesh, Bihar, and Madhya Pradesh to strengthen adaptation planning in agriculture (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/88235*).

• **CCAFS** synthesized national climate adaptation plans for India, focusing on agriculture sector adaptation measures (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959*).

• **CGIAR researchers** developed smartphone-based near-surface remote sensing to monitor crop growth disturbances and production risks in northwest India, supporting food security and risk management (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DBAFZY*).

• **CGIAR** assessed COVID-19 lockdown impacts on agricultural markets in Haryana, analyzing crop losses, borrowing, and food security disruptions (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PAWM4U*).
India.mitigation: • **CCAFS/CIMMYT** designed Climate-Smart Villages interventions to reduce greenhouse gas emissions while improving productivity in Haryana (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/42346*).
India.water:
• **CGIAR** facilitated experiential learning tools for sustainable groundwater and surface water governance in Andhra Pradesh, Rajasthan, and Karnataka, fostering collective action and informed decision-making (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/8EGMOT*).

• **TAFSSA** assessed water access, groundwater use, and land-water resource management in Nalanda district through household and community surveys (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WQBKVH*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/W50RXR*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2YQNRF*).

Uganda.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, policy coherence, and gender-inclusive adaptation** in **Uganda** through evidence-based research and policy analysis. Specifically:
- **CIAT** assessed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) interventions to reduce economic losses in soybean and sunflower production (Sources: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103239*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107720*).
- **CCAFS** conducted gender-disaggregated surveys to analyze climate adaptation strategies and coping mechanisms in Rakai (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/CBVLK5*).
- **CGIAR researchers** evaluated Uganda’s **National Adaptation Programme of Action (NAPA)** and policy frameworks to strengthen community resilience (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79935*).
Uganda.adaptation:
• **CGIAR** reviewed **Eastern Africa’s climate change policies** (including Uganda) to identify gaps in integrating climate adaptation with agriculture and food security, emphasizing the need for stronger coordination among stakeholders (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119747*).

• **CIAT & IITA** analyzed **Uganda’s NAPA implementation**, highlighting challenges in capacity building, gender-sensitive adaptation, and multi-sectoral resilience strategies (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79935*).

• **CCAFS** conducted a **gender-focused climate survey in Rakai**, documenting disparities in asset access, climate perceptions, and adaptation strategies between men and women (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/CBVLK5*).

• **CGIAR** assessed **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) for soybeans and sunflowers**, projecting CSA’s potential to reduce US$1.5 billion in projected climate-related losses by 2050 (Sources: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103239*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107720*).

• **CGIAR** linked **climate change to youth migration risks** in East Africa, modeling correlations between drought frequency and outmigration trends (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278*).

• **CGIAR** evaluated **policy and institutional frameworks** in Uganda to assess their impact on grassroots climate adaptation and gender equity (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67156*).
Uganda.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** analyzed **livestock sector policies in Kenya, Ethiopia, and Uganda**, identifying gaps in mitigation strategies and adaptation-mitigation synergies, with Ethiopia showing the most policy coherence for livestock GHG reduction (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101262*).

• **CGIAR** framed **climate-smart cocoa (CSC) interventions** (though primarily in Ghana) as a model for **GHG mitigation** in agriculture, with potential applicability to Uganda’s crop systems (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103770*).

Colombia.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers in Colombia advanced climate-resilient agricultural systems through targeted innovations in adaptation, mitigation, and agroecological resilience. Specifically:
- **CIAT** led physiological and genetic studies to enhance crop resilience under stress (e.g., drought, soil pH) and shade tolerance for silvopastoral systems.
- **CCAFS** and **Bioversity** developed climate-suitability models, gender-disaggregated vulnerability assessments, and sustainable land-use frameworks to strengthen food security and livelihoods.
- **CIAT** and **Clima-LoCa** partnered to map agroclimatic zones for cocoa and integrate climate risks into supply-chain strategies.
Colombia.adaptation:
• **CIAT** generated datasets on *Canavalia brasiliensis* genotypes to evaluate physiological, morphological, and productive responses to drought and soil pH interactions, informing genetic variability and phenotypic plasticity for forage resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HWLPUK).

• **Clima-LoCa (CGIAR-CCAFS)** identified future cocoa agroclimatic zones (ZAC) in Colombia, projecting suitability shifts under climate change (2040–2080) to guide adaptive land-use planning (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/125818).

• **CIAT** modeled climate impacts on cocoa suitability and impact gradients for Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru, providing spatial tools for risk assessment (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LXBZMU).

• **CCAFS** assessed climate vulnerability and adaptive capacity in Bogotá’s smallholder livelihoods using a sustainable rural livelihoods framework (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HA22TC).

• **CIAT** analyzed morpho-physiological traits of *Urochloa* hybrids under shade to identify constitutive/plastic traits for silvopastoral system deployment (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FOHK4X).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed climate-yield models for PepsiCo’s potato supply chain, integrating adaptation practices via the Climate Resilience Platform (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422).

• **CIAT** conducted genomic prediction of common bean agronomic traits under drought and phosphorus stress, enabling stress-tolerant breeding (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XCD67U).

• **CCAFS** conducted gender-disaggregated surveys in Climate Smart Villages to quantify climate vulnerability, risk perception, and CSA adoption barriers (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/28324).
Colombia.mitigation: • **CIAT** conducted socioeconomic surveys in Caquetá and Cesar to characterize cacao agroforestry systems, documenting adoption barriers and enabling conditions for sustainable land-use systems (SLUS) as climate mitigation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/G76RMO).

Vietnam.narrative:
CGIAR’s Research Programs prioritized climate-smart agriculture in Vietnam through gender-inclusive adaptation strategies, mitigation-focused policy analysis, and integrated water-resource management. Specifically:
- The **Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** program advanced participatory vulnerability assessments and gender-responsive adaptation measures in the Mekong Delta (Source_1).
- **CCAFS and IRRI** developed climate-smart maps and adaptation plans for 13 Mekong Delta provinces, addressing flooding, salinity, and hydrological risks (Source_8).
- **IRRI** validated Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) rice practices, reducing water use by 30% and methane emissions by 48% (Source_6).
Vietnam.adaptation:
• **CCAFS** analyzed gender differences in climate change perception and adaptation strategies among rice farmers in Vietnam’s Mekong Delta, identifying resource-access gaps and participatory action research methods (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100189, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76928, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/70965).
• **CCAFS and IRRI** created Climate-Smart Maps and Adaptation Plans (CS MAP) for 13 Mekong Delta provinces, integrating local knowledge with technical data to design adaptive rice calendars and hydrological management schemes (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100093, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115806).
• **IRRI** developed pest-smart early warning systems and interventions to mitigate climate-induced pest/disease risks in rice production (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97537).
Vietnam.mitigation:
• **CCAFS** assessed challenges in implementing Vietnam’s NDC agriculture activities, highlighting gaps in technical capacity, coordination, and private-sector engagement (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/90410).
• **IRRI** demonstrated Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) rice practices, reducing methane emissions by 48% while maintaining yields (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/35591).
• **CCAFS** explored mitigation and adaptation options for smallholder farmers in Southeast Asia, including greenhouse gas reduction strategies for agricultural lands (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003).
Vietnam.water:
• **CCAFS and IRRI** integrated water-resource management into adaptation plans, addressing flooding, salinity intrusion, and hydrological risks in the Mekong Delta (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100093).
• **IRRI** promoted AWD practices, reducing water use by up to 30% in irrigated rice systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/35591).
• **CCAFS** provided daily climate data (precipitation/temperature) to inform water-related vulnerability assessments (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2LRIOU).

Ghana.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA), gender-responsive adaptation, and climate finance mobilization** in **Ghana** through policy frameworks, farmer resilience strategies, and economic cost-benefit analyses. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** advanced **gender-inclusive CSA practices** to address productivity gaps and vulnerability disparities (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111543**).
- **CCAFS and partners** designed **location-specific adaptation plans** (e.g., National CSA Action Plan) to enhance climate-resilient cocoa production and agroecological zone targeting (Sources: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69000**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/5w7lgw**).
Ghana.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** analyzed **gender-specific adaptation capacities** in CSA, highlighting disparities in resource access and adoption barriers for women in climate-vulnerable regions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111543).

• **CCAFS and AICCRA** developed **climate-resilient cocoa production strategies**, including shade-tree adaptation to mitigate drought stress and extreme weather (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/5w7lgw, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103770).

• **CGIAR’s West Africa program** supported **national adaptation planning** (e.g., Ghana’s 2016–2020 CSA Action Plan) through participatory stakeholder workshops and policy alignment (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69000, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959).

• **CCAFS conducted cost-benefit analyses** of CSA practices in Ghana’s Coastal Savannah agroecological zone, quantifying productivity gains and operational costs (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Q6BO6Q).

• **CGIAR harmonized weather-variability data** to assess impacts on household welfare, linking precipitation/temperature trends to agroecological vulnerability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WP4PFG).

• **CCAFS evaluated economic incentives** for climate adaptation in West African cocoa, framing adaptation investments as cost-effective resilience measures (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97166).

• **CGIAR explored opportunity crops** as adaptive solutions for food security under changing climates (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZFVCU9).
Ghana.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** integrated **GHG mitigation** into Climate-Smart Cocoa frameworks, targeting reduced emissions alongside resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103770).

• **AICCRA capacitated West African CSOs** (including Ghana) to mobilize climate finance (e.g., Green Climate Fund) for locally relevant mitigation and adaptation actions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563).

Bangladesh.narrative:
CGIAR’s **Transforming Agrifood Systems in South Asia (TAFSSA)** initiative and **Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** research program prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive agrifood systems** in Bangladesh through gender-inclusive, district-level assessments and targeted adaptation surveys. Specifically:
- **TAFSSA** conducted multi-stakeholder (household, market, community) assessments linking climate risk responses, natural resource management, and agrifood system dynamics (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZDZVIZ*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QZL8NH*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S8LYJR*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VSPJKH*).
- **CCAFS** and partners led longitudinal surveys (BCCAS, Gender and Climate Change Surveys) to document adaptation strategies, climatic shocks, and resilience gaps across agroecological zones (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/27883*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VPTJZY*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/27704*).
Bangladesh.adaptation:
• **TAFSSA** assessed climate risk responses, resilience, and adaptation measures through household (male/female/adolescent) and community surveys in Rangpur and Rajshahi districts, integrating gender, asset ownership, and decision-making dynamics (*https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZDZVIZ*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QZL8NH*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VSPJKH*).

• **CCAFS** conducted the **Bangladesh Climate Change Adaptation Survey (BCCAS)** (Rounds I & II) to track adaptation options, climatic shocks, and changes in agricultural practices across 7 agroecological zones (*https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/27883*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/27704*).

• **IFPRI-CCAFS** explored gender-disaggregated climate perceptions, coping strategies, and constraints to adaptation in Bagerhat and Satkhira, linking weather information access to resilience (*https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VPTJZY*).

• **CGIAR** synthesized national adaptation plans (NAPs) for Bangladesh, focusing on agriculture-sector resilience and cross-sectoral linkages (e.g., water, land use) (*https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959*).

• **CCAFS** documented **10 climate-resilient agriculture success stories** in South Asia, including Bangladesh, highlighting transformative practices to protect farming systems (*https://hdl.handle.net/10568/83477*).
Bangladesh.water:
• **TAFSSA** integrated **water access, groundwater use, and land management** into district-level assessments, analyzing household water security, market food-sourcing resilience, and community-level groundwater dynamics (*https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZDZVIZ*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S8LYJR*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VSPJKH*).

• **TAFSSA’s market surveys** examined water-related food system vulnerabilities, including water-dependent crop availability and wastage in multi-vendor markets (*https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S8LYJR*).

Tanzania.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and resilience-building** in **Tanzania** through policy integration, crop-specific risk assessments, and adaptation planning. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) and Climate Information Services (CIS) modules** for university curricula, enhancing faculty and student engagement in climate-resilient practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179613).
- **CGIAR researchers** conducted **policy reviews** to strengthen institutional coordination between climate change, agriculture, and food security stakeholders (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119747).
Tanzania.adaptation:
• **CIAT** documented the **adoption of CSA and CIS modules** in Tanzanian universities, scaling climate-resilient agricultural education through RUFORUM and WASCAL networks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179613).
• **CGIAR** reviewed **national policies and frameworks** in Tanzania, identifying gaps in budgeting, planning, and cross-sectoral coordination for climate adaptation in agriculture (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119747).
• **CIAT** assessed **climate risks for common beans**, highlighting nitrogen fixation resilience and yield challenges under changing conditions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103235).
• **CGIAR** compiled **success stories of Climate-Smart Villages**, showcasing scalable CSA interventions for smallholder farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/81030).
• **CGIAR** analyzed the **nexus of climate change, agriculture, and youth migration**, projecting increased vulnerability due to droughts and livelihood pressures (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278).
• **CGIAR** synthesized **national adaptation plans** for Tanzania, focusing on agriculture sector strategies under the UNFCCC’s NAP process (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959).
• **CGIAR** examined **climate adaptation and mitigation initiatives**, emphasizing irrigation, capacity building, and private-public partnerships for resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/35083).
• **CIAT** conducted a **climate risk assessment for sorghum**, identifying stagnated yields due to lack of commercial markets and access to improved varieties (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107719).
• **CIAT** evaluated **climate risks for sunflower**, noting potential for productivity gains despite Tanzania’s status as a net importer of edible cooking oil (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103237).
• **CGIAR** harmonized **weather variability data** to measure impacts on household welfare across agroecological zones in Tanzania (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WP4PFG).
Tanzania.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** included **climate-smart agriculture interventions** in East Africa that dual-purpose adaptation and mitigation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/81030).
• **CGIAR** highlighted **irrigation and private-public partnerships** as mitigation-adaptation strategies in Tanzania’s agricultural sector (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/35083).

Philippines.narrative:
CGIAR’s Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) and partners prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA), gender-inclusive adaptation, and policy coherence** in the **Philippines** through participatory research, technology compendia, and localized interventions. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** advanced **gender-responsive adaptation** by analyzing vulnerabilities and coping mechanisms in Southeast Asian agriculture (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100189).
- **CCAFS SEA** compiled **climate-resilient agriculture (CRA) technologies** tailored to the Philippines’ agro-ecological zones, enhancing adaptive capacity and reducing GHG emissions (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106136).
Philippines.adaptation:
• **CCAFS** analyzed **gender dimensions in climate change adaptation**, identifying resource gaps (e.g., access to information, finance) that constrain women and men farmers’ adoption of climate-smart practices, while demonstrating participatory vulnerability assessments (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100189).

• **CCAFS SEA** developed a **Compendium of Climate-Resilient Agriculture Technologies** for the Philippines, grouping CRA options by agro-ecological suitability to improve productivity and adaptive capacity while reducing GHG emissions (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106136).

• **CCAFS** modeled **climate impacts on rice yield** (0.24–0.9% decline) and projected food demand shifts (e.g., 85,000 mt less rice demand by 2030) under RCP 8.5, informing risk management strategies (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116413).

• **CCAFS/IIRR** established **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV) in Guinayangan, Quezon**, serving as a scalable model for testing and disseminating climate-smart technologies, with outcomes harvested to inform national policies (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116444).

• **CCAFS** conducted **municipality-level climate risk mapping** to prioritize crop diversification in high-risk zones, integrating hazard and socio-economic data for targeted adaptation planning (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108583).

• **CCAFS** supported the **Adaptation and Mitigation Initiative for Agriculture (AMIA) Program**, documenting lessons from Climate-Smart Villages to strengthen local resilience-building platforms (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106787).

United States.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and conservation** in the **United States** through targeted research on crop wild relatives and climate-smart adaptation frameworks. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Bioversity International** developed a national inventory of **crop wild relatives (CWR)** in the U.S., conducting **threat assessments** and **conservation gap analyses** to inform prioritization and action (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BV4I06**).
- **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience Platform** (via PepsiCo collaboration) modeled **climate-yield risks** for potato supply chains, integrating actionable adaptation practices into sustainability roadmaps (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422**).
United States.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience Platform** (collaborating with PepsiCo) created **climate-yield models** to assess risks and opportunities in potato production, enabling data-driven adaptation practices across 12 countries, including U.S. supply chains (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422).
• **CGIAR’s Bioversity International** conducted **ecogeographic threat assessments** for U.S. crop wild relatives, identifying **conservation gaps** to strengthen resilience against climate variability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BV4I06).
• **CGIAR’s Climate Information Services Research Initiative** (via USAID funding) advanced methodologies to improve **farmer-focused climate data delivery**, addressing decision-making needs for climate risk management (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111098).
United States.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CCAFS (Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security)** piloted **low-emission development options** for large-scale dairy farms in **China** (as part of a broader CGIAR-led initiative), though no direct U.S.-focused mitigation outputs were identified in this sample (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111194).

Senegal.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation** in **Senegal** through gender-inclusive research, farmer-centric climate services, and evidence-based policy support. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s CCAFS** conducted gender-disaggregated surveys to analyze climate perceptions, asset disparities, and adaptation strategies among rural households in Kaffrine (Senegal) (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VA2MER).
- **CGIAR-CCAFS** collaborated with ANACIM to develop **seasonal rainfall forecasts** disseminated via rural radio and SMS, reaching 7.4 million farmers nationwide (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68144).
- **Bioversity International and CIAT** implemented **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) monitoring** in Kaffrine to assess smallholder adaptation to climate shocks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111561).
Senegal.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** led a **gender-focused survey** in Kaffrine to document climate shocks, coping strategies, and asset-based vulnerabilities among men and women, informing resilience-building strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VA2MER).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** partnered with Senegal’s **National Meteorological Agency (ANACIM)** to design **climate information services**, integrating farmer feedback to deliver seasonal forecasts as a production input (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68144).

• **CGIAR’s AICCRA** analyzed **social networks** in Linguère, revealing informal channels (community radios, peers) as critical for disseminating climate adaptation knowledge among livestock farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/177549).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** synthesized **national adaptation plans** across West Africa, including Senegal, to align agricultural sector strategies with UNFCCC’s NAP process (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959).

• **Bioversity International and CIAT** evaluated **CSA practices** in Kaffrine’s Climate-Smart Village, linking monitoring data to large-scale resilience programs (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111561).

• **CGIAR** conducted a **gender household survey** in Senegal to assess climate risk perception and decision-making disparities, aiming to reduce vulnerabilities (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UJENX3).
Senegal.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s AICCRA** trained **West African CSOs** (including Senegalese networks) to mobilize climate finance via **Green Climate Fund (GCF) concept notes**, addressing barriers to mitigation and adaptation funding (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563).

Guatemala.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation** in **Guatemala** through multi-disciplinary frameworks, gender-inclusive policies, and agroecological innovations. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** developed an integrated vulnerability assessment framework to project climate suitability for coffee and maize/bean systems, identifying adaptation strategies for smallholder families (Source: [https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/29418](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/29418)).
- **CCAFS** and **MAGA** co-designed gender-sensitive climate-smart agriculture (CSA) guidelines, mainstreaming gender equality into policy and monitoring systems (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108323](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108323)).
Guatemala.adaptation:
• **CCAFS** assessed climate change impacts on maize, beans, and coffee yields (projected 14–35% losses by 2050) and recommended policy actions for farmer adaptation (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/146593](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/146593)).

• **CCAFS** promoted agroecological practices (e.g., mulching, living barriers) in Guatemala’s Dry Corridor to enhance soil moisture retention and erosion control, reducing climate hazards for smallholders (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/172938](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/172938)).

• **CCAFS** conducted participatory climate mapping in Guatemala’s Agroclimatic Technical Tables (MTA) to align meteorological data with local farming systems (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111386](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111386)).

• **CCAFS** implemented an annual monitoring framework in Olopa Climate-Smart Village to track CSA adoption, food security, and climate vulnerability at household/farm levels using a standardized app (Geofarmer) (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VTPO4U](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VTPO4U)).

• **CCAFS** analyzed COVID-19’s socio-economic impacts on rural agricultural households (Huehuetenango, Quiché, San Marcos) to inform resilience strategies (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/H99OW4](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/H99OW4)).

• **CCAFS** provided sensor-based data (climate/soil) from digital maize plots (Chiquimula) to improve productivity and sustainability through digital technologies (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DGLBH7](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DGLBH7)).

• **CCAFS** proposed methods to evaluate climate change impacts on food/nutrition security in Central America, including Guatemala (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80948](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80948)).

• **CCAFS** shared insights on scaling index-based agricultural insurance for vulnerable populations, leveraging CSA principles (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472)).

Peru.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems in Peru** through targeted research on cocoa, rice, coffee, and broader land-use strategies. Specifically:
- **CIAT/CGIAR** mapped agroclimatic suitability and impact gradients for cocoa under future climate scenarios (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LXBZMU).
- **CCAFS/CGIAR** conducted climate hazard-based prioritization of Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) practices for cocoa, integrating cost-benefit analyses to guide adaptation (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/93343).
Peru.adaptation:
• **CGIAR/CCAFS** developed **climate hazard-based Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) prioritization** for cocoa in Peru, evaluating adaptation potential and profitability of practices like sustainable intensification and forest conservation to reduce land conversion (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/93343).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **spatially heterogeneous climate impacts on coffee and cocoa value chains**, proposing resilience strategies for Peru’s production systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79875, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79876).

• **CGIAR** assessed **climate change adaptation among Andean rice farmers**, examining gendered perceptions of climatic shifts and multivariate adaptation practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110692).

• **CGIAR** projected **climate change effects on Peruvian agriculture**, identifying region-specific yield changes (e.g., improved rice/wheat yields but reduced sugarcane/rainfed maize) and resilience measures for livestock and coffee (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133213).

• **CGIAR/CCAFS** scaled **Climate Risk Management (CRM) strategies** in Peru, reaching >500,000 farmers through climate services for decision-making and community resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121217).

• **CGIAR** provided **scientific evidence for sustainable land-use decisions** in Amazonian landscapes (Peru/Colombia), supporting adaptation via the **Sustainable Amazonian Landscapes (SAL) Project** (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BTRJKD).
Peru.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** integrated **GHG emission reduction** into Climate-Smart Agriculture frameworks for cocoa, emphasizing sustainable intensification to curb deforestation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/93343).

• **CGIAR** contributed to **Amazon-wide land-use databases** linking agricultural expansion to carbon/water feedbacks, with applications for mitigation planning in Peru (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J9WVY).

• **CGIAR** advanced **Agroecosystems and Sustainable Landscapes (ASL) approaches** to balance mitigation (e.g., carbon sequestration) and adaptation in Peru’s agricultural systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BTRJKD).

Mali.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate adaptation, mitigation, and gender-responsive resilience** in **Mali** through capacity-building, policy dialogue, and climate-smart agricultural innovations. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** strengthened climate finance mobilization for West African CSOs, including Mali, to access Green Climate Fund (GCF) resources (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563**).
- **CCAFS** analyzed national adaptation plans in Mali to inform agriculture-sector climate resilience (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959**).
- **CCAFS** designed a **Gender and Climate Change Action Plan for Mali**, integrating gender-responsive adaptation, community engagement, and financial mechanisms for vulnerable groups (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169250**).
Mali.adaptation:
• **CCAFS** conducted a **meta-synthesis of national climate adaptation plans** in Mali, focusing on agriculture-sector strategies and cross-sectoral linkages (e.g., water, forests) to guide policy implementation (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959**).

• **CCAFS** developed a **Gender and Climate Change Action Plan for Mali**, outlining gender-responsive adaptation measures—including community engagement, financial mechanisms, and monitoring frameworks—to address disproportionate climate vulnerabilities among women, youth, and marginalized groups (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169250**).

• **CCAFS** facilitated **science-policy dialogue platforms** (e.g., CASA C-platform) in Mali to bridge researcher-policymaker gaps, improving climate-smart agriculture (CSA) uptake through evidence-based advocacy (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/56842**).

• **CCAFS** documented **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV) practices** in West Africa, including Mali, to mainstream CSA techniques like intercropping, sustainable intensification, and integrated tree-cropping systems (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99413**).

• **CCAFS** analyzed **household coping strategies** in Mali (2018–2023) through integrated food security datasets, linking extreme weather events (e.g., droughts, floods) to adaptive behaviors and policy-relevant insights (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NNAYAM**).

• **CCAFS** explored **gender perceptions of climate adaptation** in West Africa, mainstreaming social differentiation into research activities through capacity-building and women’s empowerment (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/98394**).

• **CCAFS** provided **scaling insights for climate-smart agricultural insurance** (e.g., index-based schemes) to enhance resilience for vulnerable smallholder farmers in Mali, based on global South-South collaboration learnings (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472**).

• **CCAFS** evaluated **science-policy interactions** in Mali’s National Science-Policy Dialogue Platform (NSPDP-CSA), recommending frameworks to align CSA evidence with national priorities and improve policy uptake (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101145**).
Mali.mitigation: • **CCAFS** supported **climate finance mobilization** in Mali by training West African CSOs (via the iGST WA Hub) to develop bankable Green Climate Fund (GCF) concept notes, linking adaptation *and* mitigation actions to global funding mechanisms (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563**).

Mexico.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation** in **Mexico** through integrated vulnerability assessments, agroforestry innovation, and supply-chain climate-risk modeling. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **climate suitability models** for coffee and cocoa agroforestry systems to evaluate future vulnerability and abiotic stress mitigation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/0O1GW1).
- **CIP/CIAT** designed **genetic evaluation schemes** (VEF-EP-IBYAN) to distribute drought-tolerant bean varieties regionally, including Mexico’s semiarid zones (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S7LJBI).
- **AgriLAC Resiliente** synthesized **country-specific adaptation priorities** for Mexico within a regional climate-resilience framework (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116177).
Mexico.adaptation:
• **CIAT** created an **integrated vulnerability framework** for coffee-growing families in Mexico (and Mesoamerica), combining climate models and livelihood assessments to project exposure and adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/29418).
• **CIAT** published an **impact gradient atlas** for Mexico’s coffee zones, mapping agroclimatic shifts and adaptation needs to guide sustainable investments (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/104019).
• **CIAT** assessed **cocoa and agroforestry tree suitability** under climate change via Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) to inform diversification strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/0O1GW1).
• **PepsiCo/CGIAR collaboration** developed the **Climate Resilience Platform**, using potato-climate yield models to prioritize adaptation practices in Mexico’s supply chains (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422).
• **MTA (Mesas Técnicas Agroclimáticas)** facilitated **multi-stakeholder dialogues** in Mexico to generate climate-risk reduction recommendations for farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108780).
• **CIAT** presented **projected climate impacts on coffee value chains** at the Latin American Coffee Summit (Mexico City), proposing resilience strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79885).
• **CIAT/Bean Program** distributed **drought-tolerant bean germplasm** through the VEF(IBN)-EP-IBYAN scheme, targeting Mexico’s semiarid regions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S7LJBI).
• **AgriLAC Resiliente** identified **Mexico-specific adaptation priorities** within a regional resilience initiative (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116177).
Mexico.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** contributed to **poverty mapping** in Mexico, linking spatial food security data to climate vulnerability to inform mitigation-linked resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DQQIXZ).
• **AgriLAC Resiliente** integrated **climate change mitigation** alongside adaptation as a priority theme for Mexico (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116177).

Honduras.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers prioritized **climate resilience, agricultural productivity, and water resource planning** in **Honduras** through **data-driven tools, agroecological adaptation, and climate scenario modeling**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **high-resolution climate surfaces and future scenarios** to support policy analysis (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/E3C1KB](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/E3C1KB)).
- **CGIAR-CCAFS** assessed **crop productivity shocks** and **agroecological resilience** to inform adaptation strategies (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133215](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133215), [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/172938](https://doi.org/10.5683/SP2/AHQZ9X)).
- **CIAT** mapped **soil properties and flood risks** to enhance water resource planning (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QVXA7U](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/QVXA7U), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UKW0QT](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/UKW0QT)).
Honduras.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** modeled **crop-specific productivity losses** (e.g., 12% for maize, 10% for beans) due to climate change and recommended **policy interventions** to incentivize farmer adaptation, with a focus on **coffee and livestock resilience** in southern Honduras ([https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133215](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133215)).

• **CGIAR** promoted **agroecological practices** (e.g., mulch, living barriers) in the **Dry Corridor** to **increase soil moisture, reduce erosion, and enhance climate resilience**, while emphasizing **low-cost, scalable solutions** for smallholders ([https://hdl.handle.net/10568/172938](https://doi.org/10.5683/SP2/AHQZ9X)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** proposed **methods to measure climate impacts on food and nutrition security** in Central America, linking **climate variability to agricultural vulnerability** ([https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80948](https://doi.org/10.5683/SP2/9QJX5Y)).

• **CGIAR** developed **agroclimatic indices** (e.g., SPEI, heatwave days) to track **dry conditions, El Niño/La Niña impacts**, and **water availability**, providing seasonal insights for adaptation planning ([https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JZTN8Z](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/JZTN8Z)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** explored **scaling climate-smart agricultural insurance** for vulnerable populations, highlighting **index-based insurance schemes** as tools to build resilience ([https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472](https://doi.org/10.5683/SP2/9QJX5Y)).
Honduras.water:
• **CIAT** generated **digital soil property maps** (e.g., field capacity, water-holding capacity) at **30m resolution** for western Honduras to support the **AGRI tool**, which identifies **suitable water sources for small-scale irrigation** ([https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QVXA7U](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/QVXA7U)).

• **CGIAR** created **flood detection datasets** (2017–2022) using **remote sensing**, classifying annual flood events to inform **water resource planning** ([https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UKW0QT](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/UKW0QT)).

• **CIAT** developed **historical and future climate surfaces** (precipitation, temperature) at **1km resolution** to support **hydrological modeling and policy decisions** ([https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/YR7QYP](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/YR7QYP), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ)).

• **CGIAR** integrated **climate surfaces (precipitation, temperature)** into **adaptation frameworks**, enabling **decision-making for water management** ([https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ](https://doi.org/10.7910/DVN/QET5UQ)).

Burkina Faso.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture, policy coherence, and integrated value chains** in Burkina Faso through **adaptation-focused interventions, threat mapping, and livestock-climate policy analysis**. Specifically:
- **CIAT, IWMI, and IFPRI** evaluated **land and water management practices** for climate resilience (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).
- **Bioversity International** developed **spatially explicit threat maps** for 16 food tree species to assess climate and land-use risks (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/3BTC8J).
- **CCAFS** analyzed **national climate adaptation plans** in Burkina Faso’s agriculture sector (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959).
- **AICCRA** trained **West African CSOs** (including Burkina Faso) to mobilize climate finance for locally relevant adaptation and mitigation (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563).
- **CGIAR-led SELEVER study** (CIAT/IFPRI/CIAT partners) integrated **poultry value chains, women’s empowerment, and hygiene interventions** to adapt to climate variability (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M4BTGB, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/E7QTZZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SFGXCE, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/X7RRHL).
- **CCAFS** conducted a **policy coherence analysis** to align livestock adaptation and mitigation strategies in Burkina Faso (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108732).
Burkina Faso.adaptation:
• **CIAT, IWMI, and IFPRI** assessed **integrated land and water management practices** to enhance smallholder resilience to climate variability in Burkina Faso (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

• **Bioversity International** generated **spatially explicit threat maps** for 16 food tree species, quantifying risks from climate change, overexploitation, and land-use pressures (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/3BTC8J).

• **CCAFS** synthesized **national climate adaptation plans** in Burkina Faso, focusing on agriculture sector resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959).

• **CGIAR-led SELEVER study (CIAT/IFPRI/CIAT partners)** implemented an **integrated poultry value chain intervention** combining production systems, women’s empowerment, and hygiene education to adapt to climate-induced lean seasons (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M4BTGB, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/E7QTZZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SFGXCE, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/X7RRHL).

• **CCAFS** analyzed **livestock adaptation strategies** in Burkina Faso, highlighting farmer shifts to dual agriculture-livestock systems under climate stress (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/12422).
Burkina Faso.mitigation:
• **AICCRA** capacitated **West African CSOs (including Burkina Faso)** to access climate finance (e.g., Green Climate Fund) for locally relevant **adaptation and mitigation actions**, including livestock and agriculture sector interventions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563).

• **CCAFS** conducted a **policy coherence analysis** in Burkina Faso to identify gaps in livestock sector mitigation strategies, emphasizing the need for **GHG emission reduction targets** and **adaptation-mitigation co-benefits** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108732).
Burkina Faso.water:
• **CIAT, IWMI, and IFPRI** evaluated **land and water management interventions** as part of climate adaptation strategies, though no standalone water-focused innovations were identified in this sample (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

• **CGIAR-led SELEVER study** indirectly addressed **water-related hygiene practices** within its poultry value chain intervention (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M4BTGB, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/E7QTZZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SFGXCE, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/X7RRHL).

Rwanda.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture, policy coherence, and sustainable natural resource management** in **Rwanda** through **data-driven interventions, climate services, and livestock policy analysis**. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** advanced **climate-smart agriculture (CSA)** and **climate information services** to enhance farmer resilience and productivity (Sources: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119747*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ESK6BB*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/83098*).
- **CIAT, IRI, and Meteo Rwanda** launched **climate services for agriculture**, providing **timely climate risk management tools** to **1 million+ farmers** (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/98232*).
- **CCAFS and USAID** conducted **deep-dive assessments** of CSA integration in **Feed the Future projects**, identifying gaps and opportunities for scaling climate-adaptive practices (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/81015*).
Rwanda.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** conducted a **policy review** of climate change, agriculture, and food/nutrition security frameworks in **Eastern Africa (including Rwanda)**, highlighting gaps in **budgeting, monitoring, and stakeholder coordination** to strengthen climate-resilient agricultural productivity (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119747*).

• **CGIAR (via NISR’s Seasonal Agriculture Survey data)** analyzed the **impact of fertilizer subsidies on crop yields** (2017–2020), providing evidence for **sustainable agricultural productivity** across **76,182 plots** in Rwanda (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SMGIBM*).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS, CIAT, and IRI** launched the **Rwanda Climate Services for Agriculture project**, delivering **climate information services** to rural farmers for **improved risk management** (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/98232*).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** evaluated **farmer awareness of climate services** (2016 survey of **3,046 farmers**), identifying needs for **better climate information dissemination** (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/83098*).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** assessed **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) integration** in **USAID’s Feed the Future portfolio**, recommending scaling of adaptive practices in Rwanda’s agricultural sector (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/81015*).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed a **strategy for East Africa (2019–2021)** to support **food/nutrition security under climate change**, emphasizing **sustainable natural resource management** (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99040*).

• **CGIAR (IFAD-CSD project)** conducted a **socio-economic baseline survey** on **Climate-Smart Dairy systems**, focusing on **livestock feeding strategies and resilience** in Rwanda’s Southern Highlands (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9GBKPW*).
Rwanda.mitigation: • **CGIAR** analyzed **policy coherence for livestock climate action** in Rwanda (and 4 other countries), identifying gaps in **GHG emission reduction strategies** and **adaptation-mitigation co-benefits** to enhance sector resilience (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108732*).
Rwanda.water:
• **CGIAR (CIAT)** generated **current/future forage suitability maps** for Rwanda using **climate (precipitation, temperature) and soil data**, supporting **climate-resilient forage systems** (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ESK6BB*).

• **CGIAR (World Agroforestry/ICRAF)** applied the **Land Degradation Surveillance Framework (LDSF)** in **eastern Rwanda**, assessing **land/soil health drivers** and degradation surveillance (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/NMYOFA*).

Nepal.narrative:
CGIAR’s **TAFSSA (Transforming Agrifood Systems in South Asia)** and **CCAFS (Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security)** prioritized **climate-resilient agrifood systems and adaptation planning** in **Nepal** through integrated district-level assessments, policy-aligned training, and community-driven climate-smart agriculture (CSA) interventions. Specifically:
- **TAFSSA** conducted **gender-disaggregated, multi-stakeholder assessments** linking farm production, water access, and climate risk responses across Surkhet and Banke districts (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XZRK2F*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BA5X3F*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PZMNBU*).
- **CCAFS** developed **Climate-Smart Agriculture training manuals** and championed **scaling CSA technologies** tailored to Nepal’s agro-ecological zones (Sources: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111199*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/82600*).
Nepal.adaptation:
• **TAFSSA** assessed **household and community climate risk responses**, including agricultural activities, livestock, fish cultivation, and future farming aspirations, with gender as a cross-cutting theme in Surkhet and Banke districts (*https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XZRK2F*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PZMNBU*).

• **CCAFS** published a **Climate-Smart Agriculture Training Manual** to strengthen farmer capacity in adopting resilient practices, aligned with Nepal’s **National Adaptation Programme of Action (NAPA)** and **Local Adaptation Plan of Action (LAPA)** (*https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111199*).

• **CCAFS** synthesized **champion CSA technologies** for Nepal’s agro-ecological regions, addressing droughts, unseasonal rains, and temperature shifts to enhance agricultural resilience (*https://hdl.handle.net/10568/82600*).

• **CCAFS** documented **Tharu community adaptive initiatives** in Western Terai, highlighting traditional knowledge-based strategies for climate vulnerability mitigation (*https://hdl.handle.net/10568/35795*).

• **CCAFS** compiled **top success stories** of climate-resilient agriculture in South Asia, including Nepal, to demonstrate scalable models for protecting farming systems (*https://hdl.handle.net/10568/83477*).

• **CCAFS** analyzed **national adaptation planning processes** in Nepal, informing sectoral policies for agriculture, water, and land-use resilience (*https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959*).
Nepal.water:
• **TAFSSA** integrated **water resource management** into agrifood systems assessments, evaluating access, groundwater use, and land-use dynamics in Surkhet and Banke districts (*https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XZRK2F*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PZMNBU*).

• **TAFSSA** examined **retail-level food system resilience**, including water-linked factors like food sourcing and wastage in local markets (*https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BA5X3F*).

Nicaragua.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural systems** in **Nicaragua** through **adaptation frameworks, agroecological practices, and policy-informed interventions**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed **spatiotemporal adaptation frameworks** for coffee production, distinguishing incremental and transformative strategies to guide farmer responses to climate suitability shifts (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/TSUPE1).
- **CCAFS** integrated **agroecological practices** (e.g., mulching, living barriers) to enhance soil moisture retention and erosion control in the **Dry Corridor**, offering actionable recommendations for policymakers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/172938).
Nicaragua.adaptation:
• **CIAT** quantified climate change impacts on **coffee suitability and quality** in Nicaragua, designing a **two-dimensional adaptation framework** (time/space) to inform planning, with incremental/transformative strategies tailored to altitude and horizon (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/TSUPE1).

• **CCAFS** assessed **vulnerability of coffee-growing families** across Mesoamerica (including Nicaragua) using climate models and livelihood-focused tools, identifying adaptation strategies to minimize climate suitability risks (https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/29418).

• **CIAT** demonstrated **crop variety management for climate adaptation** via **farmer-led tricot trials** (10 common bean varieties tested in Nicaragua), linking yield performance to environmental variables (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).

• **CCAFS** analyzed **historical climate trends (1970–2010)** in Nicaragua, linking **deforestation, warming, and rainfall shifts** to **maize/bean yield declines** (5–12% per decade), while documenting **government-led adaptation responses** (e.g., bean migration, export controls) (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/29206).

• **CIAT** influenced **Nicaragua’s National Adaptation Plan for Agriculture**, prioritizing **smallholder coffee/cocoa livelihoods** and leveraging **USD 24.12M in IFAD investments** for climate-proofed inputs and weather information systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/35599).

• **CCAFS** evaluated **costs/benefits of Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) practices** via **baseline surveys of 180 Nicaraguan farmers**, capturing perceptions of climate risks and adoption barriers (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/6UYI0X).

• **CCAFS** proposed **scaling strategies for index-based agricultural insurance** to enhance smallholder resilience, synthesizing insights from global CSA insurance schemes (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472).
Nicaragua.water: • **CCAFS** highlighted **maize’s greater water-limitation** compared to beans due to its longer growing season, attributing yield stagnation to **declining rainfall frequency and earlier rainy season ends** in Nicaragua (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/29206).

Malawi.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and adaptive capacity** in **Malawi** through targeted research, vulnerability assessments, and participatory interventions. Specifically:
- CGIAR conducted **climate risk assessments** to prioritize commodities (maize, potatoes, beans) for agricultural development interventions (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/96184**).
- The Center implemented **conservation agriculture incentives** and **riparian land management** in the Shire River Basin to enhance watershed resilience (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/GTSEWP**).
Malawi.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed **climate change impacts on maize yields** (2040–2070) using biophysical and economic modeling in Mzimba District, integrating crop-livestock systems (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/149879**)
• The Center mapped **agro-climatological zones** and conducted **spatial suitability analyses** for rainfed crops and irrigated rice to inform adaptive land-use planning (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/RBEWFZ**)
• CGIAR developed **sustainable intensification strategies** (intercropping, water conservation, new crop varieties) for smallholder farmers in Malawi (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/H6RWOO**)
• Researchers prioritized **climate-informed decision support systems** for fish farming and capture fisheries, identifying adaptation strategies for climate risks (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J29HD**)
• CGIAR scaled **climate information services** through participatory training for extension agents and NGOs to empower on-farm adaptation (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/56840**)
• The Center analyzed **climate vulnerabilities** (cyclones, floods, droughts) and **resilience gaps** in Malawi’s rainfed agriculture, linking adaptation to economic and environmental degradation (Source: **https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136955**)
• CGIAR contributed to **Green Climate Fund (GCF) projects** by categorizing adaptive responses under the **IPCC Risk Framework** for Malawi’s agricultural sector (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ**)
Malawi.water:
• CGIAR implemented **smart subsidies for catchment conservation** in riparian areas of the Shire River Basin, monitoring land management practices and conservation agriculture adoption (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/GTSEWP**)
• Researchers analyzed **precipitation variability, soil erosion, and watershed degradation** to assess climate-induced water risks in Malawi’s rainfed systems (Source: **https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136955**)

Zambia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart adaptation and resilience-building in Zambia** through targeted research, vulnerability assessments, and innovation scaling. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) and WorldFish** led gender-inclusive resilience assessments in aquatic food systems, while **ILRI and IFPRI** piloted sustainable intensification practices (e.g., intercropping, water conservation) in crop-livestock systems (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IQXBDI*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/H6RWOO*).
- **CGIAR’s adaptation research** mapped climate risks to staple crops (maize, pulses, sorghum) and prioritized interventions via theories of change and value chain analyses (Sources: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/126808*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/96184*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111415*).
Zambia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** developed a **theories-of-change framework** for monitoring climate adaptation in Zambia, illustrated through case studies linking planning, implementation, and evaluation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/126808).

• **CGIAR and partners** conducted a **climate risk assessment** for Zambia’s agricultural value chains, prioritizing maize, pulses, and sorghum for interventions based on yield projections, dietary importance, and economic impact (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/96184).

• **CGIAR’s WorldFish** implemented a **Climate-Hotspots Gender Impact Assessment** in Luapula/Northern Provinces, scaling innovations to build resilience in aquatic food systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IQXBDI).

• **CGIAR’s Africa RISING (ILRI/IFPRI)** piloted **sustainable intensification** in Zambia (via water conservation, intercropping, and new crop varieties), though primarily documented in Ethiopia’s baseline surveys (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/H6RWOO).

• **CGIAR’s Green Innovation Centres (GIC)** analyzed climate vulnerabilities in groundnut, soybean, and milk value chains, recommending innovations to manage risks in Eastern/Southern Provinces (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111415).

• **CGIAR’s PROFISHBLUE (SADC)** surveyed **537 fish farmers/artisanal fishers** in Luapula/Southern Provinces to assess climate risks to fisheries and adaptation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J29HD).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** documented **1.3°C temperature increases** and **1.9 mm/month rainfall declines** in Zambia’s agricultural regions, framing climate-smart agriculture (CSA) as critical for adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99463).

• **CGIAR modeled future forage suitability** under 2050 climatic conditions to inform livestock adaptation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/CG7YCI).
Zambia.water:
• **CGIAR’s climate risk research** projected **national water scarcity** due to declining rainfall, threatening Zambezi, Kafue, and Luangwa River Basins—highlighting hydropower and agricultural vulnerabilities (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136956).

• **CGIAR’s Africa RISING** included **water conservation practices** as part of sustainable intensification pilots (though primarily documented in Ethiopia; Zambia’s application implied) (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/H6RWOO).

Indonesia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural innovation and resilience** in **Indonesia** through targeted research on pest management, water productivity, and greenhouse gas mitigation. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** advanced **ecologically based rodent management (EBRM)** in rice-based systems, demonstrating that media campaigns and extension support could increase yields by **0.7 t/ha** through community-led pest control (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JWUEQL).
- **CGIAR researchers** modeled **supplemental irrigation and timely planting** to boost rainfed rice productivity in Central Java, identifying deep tillage and nitrogen fertilization as key yield-enhancing strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/22798).
- **CCAFS and partners** improved **greenhouse gas (GHG) monitoring and reporting** in Indonesia, enabling evidence-based mitigation targets aligned with **Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121260).
Indonesia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** evaluated **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) scaling strategies** in Southeast Asia, including climate information services and insurance mechanisms, to reduce vulnerability in smallholder systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/90537).
• **CGIAR researchers** assessed **climate variability impacts on inequality**, finding that while variability reduces income in Indonesia, it disproportionately affects **poor, rural, and female-headed households** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111516).
• **CCAFS and national partners** implemented **Climate-Smart Villages (CSV) projects** in Southeast Asia, coordinating research on resilient agricultural systems and policy upscaling (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/90938).
• **CGIAR developed a pest risk map** for the **Cassava Mealybug** in Asia, predicting climatic suitability to inform adaptive pest management (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/27604).
