• **CCAFS hosted workshops** to identify **scalable CSA options**, emphasizing technologies and institutional partnerships for climate-resilient food systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106626).
• **CGIAR promoted EBRM policy adoption** in Indonesia, linking media campaigns to **behavioral changes in rodent control** and yield improvements (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JWUEQL).
Indonesia.mitigation:
• **CGIAR supported Indonesia in strengthening MRV (Measurement, Reporting, and Verification) systems** for agricultural GHG emissions, contributing to **NDC mitigation targets** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121260).
• **CCAFS and partners organized symposia** to explore **mitigation options for greenhouse gas emissions from agricultural lands**, including soil management and alternative cropping systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003).
Indonesia.water:
• **CGIAR compiled high-resolution daily climate data** (precipitation and temperature) for Indonesia (2000–2018) to analyze drought/heat vulnerability in agricultural systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2LRIOU).
• **Researchers modeled water productivity** in rainfed rice systems, demonstrating that **supplemental irrigation and timely planting** could stabilize yields in Central Java (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/22798).

Nigeria.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Nigeria** through targeted research, capacity-building, and policy-relevant interventions. Specifically:
- **CIAT, IWMI, and IFPRI** evaluated **integrated land and water management** projects to enhance farmer resilience to climate variability (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).
- **Cocoa and cassava/yam researchers** identified **adaptation strategies** (e.g., shade trees, pest-resistant crops, fallow/mulch practices) to mitigate climate stress in key agroecological zones (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/5W7LGW, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/151034).
- **CCAFS** designed **climate-smart insurance schemes** to reduce basis risk and improve rainfall risk coverage for Nigerian farmers (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67899).
- **ERA (Evidence for Resilient Agriculture)** compiled livestock feed experiments to optimize **productivity, weight gain, and feed conversion** for cattle, sheep, and other species (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XY9WAX).
Nigeria.adaptation:
• **CIAT, IWMI, and IFPRI** conducted a **multi-country impact assessment** of land and water management projects in Nigeria and six other West African nations, identifying **scalable resilience practices** for smallholder farmers (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

• **Cocoa researchers** analyzed climate vulnerability in Nigeria’s **forest-savanna transition zones**, recommending **shade tree adoption** and spatial production shifts to offset drought and temperature stress (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/5W7LGW).

• **CGIAR’s cassava/yam team** quantified farmer adaptation strategies (e.g., **fallow, mulch, intercropping, pest-resistant crops**) in Southern Nigeria, linking education and household size to adoption likelihood (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/151034).

• **CCAFS** improved **agricultural insurance triggers** in Nigeria by refining crop-weather relationships to reduce basis risk and expand coverage for climate-smart technology adoption (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67899).

• **ERA (Livestock Data)** synthesized **405 global studies** (20% from Nigeria) to optimize **feed composition, weight gain, and meat yield** in cattle and small ruminants under climate variability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XY9WAX).
Nigeria.mitigation: • **AICCRA (supported by CGIAR partners)** capacitated **West African CSOs**—including Nigerian networks—to mobilize **climate finance** (e.g., Green Climate Fund) for locally relevant **adaptation and mitigation actions**, with a focus on bankable concept notes (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563).

China.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, GHG mitigation, and adaptive pest/disease management** in **China** through targeted research, policy guidance, and data-driven interventions. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** developed **greenhouse gas mitigation guidelines** for dairy farmers, focusing on feed and manure management (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119591**).
- **ICON/CCAFS** analyzed **water management shifts** (e.g., non-flooded crops) to assess impacts on **disease epidemics, carbon/nitrogen budgets, and crop rotation** (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9SPT9N**).
- **One CGIAR** projected **climate adaptation benefits**, estimating **24% productivity gains** and **yield variability reduction** via climate-smart practices in China’s hazard-prone regions (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115780**).
China.adaptation:
• **One CGIAR** projected **climate adaptation benefits** for **234 million rural individuals in China**, highlighting **24% productivity increases** and **reduced yield variability** through climate-smart agricultural practices, adapted varieties, and advisory services (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115780**).

• **CCAFS/ICON** studied **disease progress (e.g., sheath blight) linked to water management shifts** (flooded → non-flooded systems) and **crop rotation**, identifying functional relationships between pathogens and environmental changes in rice-maize systems (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9SPT9N**).

• **CGIAR researchers** compiled a **long-term panel dataset (1991–2015) on pest severity, insecticide use, and land cover** in China’s cotton-growing regions, enabling climate-resilient pest management strategies (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/QVBQQQ**).

• **CGIAR** generated a **pest risk map** for **Cassava Mealybug** in Asia, modeling **climatic suitability** to inform adaptive pest vulnerability assessments (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/27604**).

• **CGIAR** framed **climate resilience strategies** for China’s agricultural food system, emphasizing **climate-smart agriculture, trade openness, and infrastructure** to cope with **changing water/heat conditions** and extreme weather (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/168919**).
China.mitigation:
• **CCAFS** created **greenhouse gas mitigation guidelines** for Chinese dairy farmers, covering **feed management, manure management, and total-farm approaches** to reduce GHG emissions (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119591**).

• **CGIAR-supported countries (including China)** improved **MRV (Measurement, Reporting, Verification) systems** for agricultural emissions, enabling **evidence-based NDC mitigation targets** (24% of annual ag emissions tracked) (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121260**).

• **CGIAR researchers** developed **Tier II MRV guidance for livestock emissions** in China, presented to the **Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences (CAAS)** for provincial-level implementation (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97861**).

• **CGIAR** conducted **carbon footprint assessments** for dairy cattle in China, presenting **mitigation options** to CAAS and the **Sino-Dutch Dairy Development Centre** (Sources: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97868**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97867**).
China.water:
• **ICON/CCAFS** investigated **water management regimes** (flooded → non-flooded crops) to evaluate impacts on **carbon/nitrogen/water budgets** and **disease dynamics** (e.g., sheath blight) in rice-dominated landscapes (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9SPT9N**).

• **CGIAR** highlighted **changing temperatures/precipitation** as critical factors altering **water and heat conditions for plant growth**, with implications for **climate-resilient agriculture** (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/168919**).

Niger.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive strategies** in **Niger** through evidence-based interventions in agriculture, policy coherence, and household-level adaptation. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** analyzed household adaptation strategies (e.g., crop diversification, drought-resilient varieties) and their impact on income and food security, revealing mixed determinants across six adaptation categories (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847](https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847)).
- **CCAFS** conducted a meta-synthesis of national climate adaptation plans in West Africa, including Niger, to assess agriculture sector adaptation planning under the UNFCCC’s NAP process (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959)).
Niger.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** quantified the effectiveness of adaptation strategies (e.g., crop diversification, income diversification) in Niger, showing adopters earned **721,526 FCFA more** and had **7–9% higher food security** than non-adopters, while documenting widespread shifts in rainfall patterns (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847](https://doi.org/10.3390/su14052847) ).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** synthesized national adaptation plans (including Niger) to evaluate agriculture sector adaptation frameworks, aligning with UNFCCC’s NAP process (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/33959)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** analyzed **policy coherence** in livestock adaptation and mitigation across Burkina Faso, Niger, Rwanda, Nepal, and Cambodia, identifying gaps in climate-resilient livestock policies and mitigation strategies (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108732](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108732)).

• **CGIAR** integrated **food security datasets (2018–2023)** for Niger, linking household coping strategies to climate hazards (e.g., extreme weather, rainy season performance) and conflict shocks (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SWJOEN](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SWJOEN)).

• **CGIAR** documented **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) practices** in West Africa’s Climate-Smart Villages, including Niger, through a 2017 inventory (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99413](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99413)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** mainstreamed **gender perspectives** into climate adaptation research in West Africa, assessing women’s perceptions of climate risks and adaptation strategies (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/98394](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/98394)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed a **training manual** for participatory, village-based CSA implementation in Niger, Benin, Burkina Faso, Ghana, and Togo (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/144087](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/144087)).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **climate hazards, vulnerability, and adaptive responses** in Niger’s Green Climate Fund (GCF) agricultural projects, categorizing data using the **IPCC Risk Framework** (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** assessed the **climate-smartness** of WAAPP projects in Niger, Benin, Guinea, Togo, and Chad, strengthening stakeholder capacity to integrate CSA (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455)).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** explored **scaling climate-smart agricultural insurance** for vulnerable populations, highlighting challenges in index-based insurance design for Niger’s smallholders (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472)).
Niger.mitigation: • **CGIAR-CCAFS** evaluated **policy gaps** in livestock sector mitigation and adaptation in Niger, emphasizing the need for coherent strategies to limit GHG emissions while enhancing climate resilience (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108732](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108732)).

Ecuador.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and food security** in **Ecuador** through data-driven adaptation strategies, soil diagnostics, and participatory climate services. Specifically:
- CGIAR developed **spatial agroclimatic impact models** for cocoa crops to assess climate vulnerability (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LXBZMU).
- The Center mapped **soil chemical fertility** to support sustainable land use and climate-adaptive farming (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PHIQXV).
- Researchers surveyed **rice adoption practices** to link modern varieties with yield improvements (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DX3F4T).
Ecuador.adaptation:
• CGIAR generated **spatial impact gradient layers** for cocoa crops in Ecuador, projecting climate suitability shifts under future scenarios (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LXBZMU).
• The Center facilitated **agroclimatic technical tables (MTA)** to foster multi-stakeholder dialogue on climate risk reduction in Ecuador and Latin America (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108780).
• Researchers documented **rice variety adoption** and management practices among 1,028 households to inform climate-resilient production strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DX3F4T).
• CGIAR contributed to **poverty mapping** in Ecuador, linking spatial food security data with climate vulnerability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DQQIXZ).
• The Center scaled **Climate Risk Management (CRM) strategies** across Latin America, including Ecuador, reaching 500,000+ farmers through climate services (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121217).
• CGIAR implemented **participatory agro-climatic services** to adapt farming decisions to unfavorable rainfall, benefiting Ecuadorian farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121372).
Ecuador.water: • CGIAR developed **digital soil fertility maps** for continental Ecuador to minimize land-use change impacts and enhance water-resilient agriculture (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PHIQXV).

Myanmar.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, resilience-building, and adaptive livelihood strategies** in **Myanmar** through **community-based interventions, data-driven decision support, and sector-specific assessments**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** advanced **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) frameworks** to reduce vulnerability in smallholder systems (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003*).
- **WorldFish (CGIAR Center)** conducted **aquaculture performance assessments** to enhance productivity and adaptive capacity among small-scale fish farmers (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/D7FZLX*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MIYZYQ*).
Myanmar.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and partners** designed **Climate-Smart Village (CSV) frameworks** to identify climate risks, evaluate CSA options, and monitor adaptive outcomes in Myanmar’s agricultural landscapes (Sources: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99016*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138911*).

• **WorldFish** analyzed **farmers’ perceptions and adaptation strategies** to climatic shocks, linking aquaculture resilience with livelihood security (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZINQUU*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MIYZYQ*).

• **CGIAR’s COVID-19 Hub** documented **adaptation and coping strategies** in fish value chains, highlighting gendered vulnerabilities and recovery mechanisms during the pandemic (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NRPVSC*).

• **CGIAR’s Rice-Fish Futures project** developed **suitability models** for rice-fish/prawn systems in the Ayeyarwady Delta, integrating climatic variables (e.g., storm events) to inform water and crop management (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/K6LKIN*).

• **CGIAR compiled actionable resources** for practitioners to implement **climate-resilient agriculture**, synthesizing field-derived experiences from Myanmar and neighboring regions (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107971*).
Myanmar.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s CCAFS** explored **mitigation options for greenhouse gas emissions** from agricultural lands in Southeast Asia, including Myanmar, as part of a broader regional symposium on climate-smart practices (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003*).
Myanmar.water:
• **CGIAR’s WorldFish and IWMI collaboration** created a **GIS database and SWAT model** to assess water availability for smallholder aquaculture ponds, supporting scalable adoption through climate-integrated decision tools (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/TV7J27*).

• **Rice-Fish Futures (CGIAR-led)** generated **suitability maps** for rice-fish systems, implicitly addressing **water management** in climate-variant scenarios (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/K6LKIN*).

Cambodia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural resilience and policy coherence** in **Cambodia** through targeted interventions in pest management, livestock sector reform, and smallholder farmer adaptation. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** developed **pest-smart early warning systems** for rice and other crops to address climate-induced pest and disease challenges (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97537**).
- **CCAFS** analyzed **policy gaps in livestock adaptation and mitigation** to enhance climate-resilient productivity while reducing GHG emissions (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108732**).
Cambodia.adaptation:
• **CCAFS** designed **pest-smart interventions and early warning systems** to mitigate climate-driven pest and disease risks in rice production, providing actionable guidance for farmers and extension agents (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97537).

• **CCAFS** conducted a **policy coherence analysis** to identify gaps in livestock sector adaptation and mitigation, proposing reforms to align policies with climate-resilient productivity and emission reduction goals (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108732).

• **CCAFS** outlined **eight guide steps for establishing Climate-Smart Villages (CSV)**, integrating climate risk assessment, stakeholder consultation, and CSA option evaluation to enhance local resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99016, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103527).

• **CCAFS** explored **climate-smart agricultural index-based insurance schemes** to bolster smallholder resilience, addressing scalability challenges for vulnerable populations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472).

• **CCAFS** monitored **fish biodiversity and biomass** in Community Fisheries Refuges (CFRs) to evaluate the effectiveness of climate-resilient interventions during dry seasons (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/YIF7FZ).
Cambodia.mitigation:
• **CCAFS** developed a **Khmer-language primer on Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA)** for local officials, addressing GHG impacts on agriculture and food security while fostering stakeholder engagement (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100335).

• **CCAFS** examined **mitigation options for GHG emissions from agricultural lands** and **adaptation strategies** to reduce climate vulnerability in Southeast Asia, including Cambodia, through regional research collaborations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003).

• **CCAFS** analyzed **policy coherence** in livestock sectors (including Cambodia) to reduce sector-wide GHG emissions while ensuring climate-resilient growth (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108732).
Cambodia.water:
• **CCAFS** investigated **water management shifts** (e.g., from flooded to non-flooded cropping systems) to assess impacts on disease progression (e.g., sheath blight) and soil-borne pathogens in rice-maize rotations (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9SPT9N).

• **CCAFS** collected **qualitative and quantitative data** on the **Tasoung Irrigation Scheme** and community fisheries (CFi/CFR) to analyze water governance in food production systems across Cambodia’s Mekong Delta and Tonle Sap Lake regions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/C1Y84W).

Brazil.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart food systems and policy advocacy** in **Brazil** through **science-based interventions and digital tools**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR centers** (e.g., ILRI, WorldFish, CIAT) advanced **food systems mitigation** via global policy submissions (e.g., Sharm el-Sheikh MWP) and **agroecological transitions** through digital tool reviews for Brazil’s beef sector (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181087*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DXPZ8G*).
- **CIAT** enhanced **drought-tolerant bean varieties** via regional germplasm evaluation schemes (VEF-EP-IBYAN) to support national adaptation programs (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S7LJBI*).
- **CGIAR** influenced **global climate finance** (e.g., NCQG negotiations) and **supply-chain resilience** (e.g., PepsiCo’s Climate Resilience Platform) to align agrifood systems with adaptation and mitigation goals (Sources: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/180376*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179970*).
Brazil.adaptation:
• **CGIAR** developed **climate-yield models** for PepsiCo’s potato supply chain, integrating adaptation practices via the **Climate Resilience Platform** to strengthen resilience across 12 countries (including Brazil) (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422*).
• **CIAT** designed the **VEF(IBN)-EP-IBYAN scheme** to distribute drought-tolerant bean germplasm regionally, including Brazil, via a structured evaluation pipeline (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S7LJBI*).
• **CGIAR** synthesized **country-level priorities** for *AgriLAC Resiliente* to guide adaptation strategies in Latin America and the Caribbean (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116177*).
• **CGIAR** engaged in **COP30 negotiations** (Belém, Brazil) to advance **gender-equitable adaptation metrics** and **agricultural transformation** (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181075*).
Brazil.mitigation:
• **CGIAR centers** (ILRI, WorldFish, partners) submitted a **joint proposal** to the **Sharm el-Sheikh MWP**, positioning **food systems** as a critical mitigation topic (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/181087*).
• **CGIAR** reviewed **digital tools** for Brazil’s beef sector to support **agroecological transitions**, assessing their role in **GHG emission reductions** and **inclusive knowledge co-creation** (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DXPZ8G*).
• **CGIAR** advocated at **SB62 (Bonn, 2025)** to integrate **food/agriculture** into global climate policies, emphasizing **climate finance gaps** (e.g., 4.3% of global funds for 1/3 of GHG emissions) (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/180376*).
• **CGIAR** contributed to **COP29–COP30 finance negotiations**, focusing on **sustainable agroecological food systems** and the **NCQG’s $1.3T target** (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179970*).
• **Researchers** compiled a **database of nitrous oxide (N₂O) emissions** from urine patches in Brazil (and LAC), including **N₂O emission factors** and soil nitrogen data (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BM0ILQ*).
• **CGIAR** analyzed **Amazon-wide land use change** (Brazil-focused) to link **water, carbon, and energy feedbacks** via harmonized agricultural census data (1950–2012) (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J9WVY*).
Brazil.water: • **Researchers** created an **Amazon-wide agricultural land use database** (1950–2012) to study **water feedbacks** from crops/pastures, with Brazil as a focal case for **hydroclimatic impacts** (Source: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J9WVY*).

South Africa.narrative: CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience, institutional capacity-building, and evidence-based adaptation** in **South Africa** through **household surveys, policy assessments, livestock productivity research, and climate-smart interventions**. Specifically:
South Africa.adaptation:
• **CGIAR** conducted the **Limpopo Basin Climate Change Adaptation Dataset**, a household survey across 794 households in 5 Water Management Areas (WMAs) to analyze agricultural patterns, crop water use, and adaptation options for policymakers (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IFCZ7R).

• **CGIAR** assessed **climate-vulnerable populations and compounding risks** in Southern Africa, evaluating how climate impacts undermine social cohesion and food systems, with recommendations for integrating peace and security into climate-responsive policies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173619).

• **CGIAR** synthesized evidence on **water-energy-food nexus vulnerabilities** in the SADC region, highlighting reduced crop/livestock production and food insecurity due to climate hazards, and advocating for regionwide, intersectional climate actions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114114).

• **CGIAR** examined **institutional frameworks** for climate, peace, and security in Southern Africa, identifying gaps in addressing intersectional challenges (e.g., resource conflicts, inequality) and proposing entry points for policy integration (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/175771).

• **CGIAR** developed the **Climate Resilience Platform** (via PepsiCo’s potato program) to model climate risks, prioritize adaptation practices, and enhance supply-chain resilience across 12 countries, including South Africa (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422).

• **CGIAR** compiled the **Evidence for Resilient Agriculture (ERA) Livestock Dataset**, geolocating 14.9% of observations in South Africa to analyze feed composition, productivity (meat yield, weight gain), and economic benefits for cattle, sheep, and other livestock (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XY9WAX).

• **CGIAR** emphasized **intersectional climate resilience**, highlighting gender disparities in vulnerability and advocating for climate-smart interventions that address structural inequalities limiting smallholder farmers’ adaptive capacity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/131154).

• **CGIAR** piloted **crop diversity strategies** in South Africa (alongside 11 other countries) to strengthen farmers’ adaptation through participatory research, climate modeling, and access to diverse crop varieties (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).

• **CGIAR** facilitated a **South-South capacity-building workshop** in Zimbabwe (2021) to enhance objective seasonal forecasting, co-produce climate information for agriculture, and strengthen institutional linkages across 15 African nations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941).
South Africa.mitigation: • **CGIAR** contributed to **attribution science capacity-building** for African climate negotiators, linking greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes and impacts to inform COP negotiations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).
South Africa.water:
• **CGIAR** integrated **water security** into climate adaptation research by analyzing crop water use, irrigation/dryland farming, and household water access in the Limpopo Basin (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IFCZ7R).

• **CGIAR** addressed **water scarcity and insecurity** as a critical climate impact, linking it to reduced agricultural productivity and food availability in Southern Africa (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114114).

Laos.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and adaptive resilience** in **Laos** through participatory vulnerability assessments, early warning systems, and institutional capacity-building. Specifically:
- **CCAFS-SEA** advanced gender-inclusive adaptation measures via Participatory Vulnerability Analysis (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100189**).
- The Center developed **pest-smart interventions and Early Warning Systems** for rice and crops under climate change (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97537**).
- CGIAR facilitated **Climate-Smart Village (CSV) frameworks** to evaluate CSA options and scale resilience (Sources: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99016**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103527**).
Laos.adaptation:
• **CCAFS-SEA** analyzed **gender dimensions in climate adaptation**, identifying resource access gaps (e.g., finance, training) that constrain adoption of CSA practices (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100189**).

• The Center designed **pest-smart manuals and Early Warning Systems** to mitigate crop losses from climate-altered pest/disease dynamics in rice systems (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97537**).

• CGIAR introduced **Climate-Smart Village (CSV) methodologies**—8-step guides for risk assessment, stakeholder consultation, and CSA portfolio development—to enhance local resilience (Sources: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99016**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103527**).

• Researchers explored **index-based insurance schemes** to bolster smallholder resilience, addressing scalability challenges for vulnerable populations (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472**).

• The **Crop Loss Assessment Monitor (CAM)** was piloted to improve climate risk management through regional collaboration (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97015**).

• CGIAR engaged **media workshops** (149+ participants) to disseminate CSA innovations, linking science to farmers and policymakers in Laos (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/70966**).
Laos.mitigation:
• **CCAFS-SEA** contributed to **greenhouse gas mitigation research** in Southeast Asia, including options for reducing agricultural emissions (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003**).

• The Center’s symposium outputs (e.g., *Making Smallholder Farmers Climate Smart*) synthesized **mitigation-adaptation synergies** for small-scale agriculture (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003**).

• CGIAR’s media engagement initiative indirectly supported **science-based mitigation adoption** by improving public understanding of low-emission agricultural practices (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/70966**).

El Salvador.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation** in **El Salvador** through **agricultural resilience, vulnerability assessment, and climate-smart policy frameworks**. Specifically:
- **CCAFS (Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security)** developed an **integrated vulnerability framework** for coffee-growing families, combining climate models and qualitative tools to project suitability and exposure risks (https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/29418).
- **CGIAR researchers** identified **high-impact climate spots** for drybeans production using DSSAT simulations, classifying hotspots, adaptation spots, and pressure zones (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HEQQIH).
- **CCAFS** scaled **climate-smart agricultural index-based insurances** to enhance smallholder resilience, addressing barriers for vulnerable populations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106472).
El Salvador.adaptation:
• **CGIAR/CCAFS** designed an **integrated framework** to assess vulnerability and adaptation strategies for coffee families in El Salvador, leveraging climate models and livelihood-focused qualitative tools (https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/29418).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **climate resilience** in the **Dry Corridor**, demonstrating how agroecological practices (mulch, shaded trees, living barriers) reduce erosion and soil temperature while increasing soil moisture for smallholders (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/172938).

• **CCAFS** projected **severe agricultural productivity losses** by 2050 (sorghum: -14%, maize: -13%, rice: -11%, sugar cane: -36%, coffee: >35% suitability loss), informing policy recommendations for climate threats (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133211).

• **CGIAR** proposed **methods to evaluate climate impacts on food/nutrition security** in Central America, offering tools for measuring interconnected risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80948).

• **CCAFS** facilitated **Local Technical Agroclimatic Committees (LTACs)** to co-produce climate information, translate forecasts into agronomic recommendations, and reduce crop losses for farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107914).

• **CGIAR** replicated **spatial yield simulations** to classify **high-impact climate spots** for drybeans, deriving actionable recommendations for adaptation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HEQQIH).

• **CCAFS** organized **Mesas Técnicas Agroclimáticas (MTA)**—multi-stakeholder dialogues—to generate climate-risk reduction strategies for farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108780).

• **CGIAR** addressed **climate security crises** in the **Dry Corridor**, linking droughts/floods to food insecurity and fragility, while recommending responses to rainfall variability (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116505).

Mozambique.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural transformation** in **Mozambique** through **adaptation-focused innovations** and **climate-informed interventions**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** and **International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT)** advanced **crop improvement, climate-smart varieties, and risk management tools** to bolster smallholder livelihoods and food security (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65984, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MOCXUN, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/FM3IMX).
- **CGIAR’s International Research Institute for Climate and Society (IRI)** and **CCAFS** expanded **climate services, index insurance, and seasonal forecasting** to enhance decision-making for farmers and institutions (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121437).
Mozambique.adaptation:
• **CGIAR (CCAFS, CIAT, IRI)** identified **climate-related risks** (e.g., cyclones, flooding, droughts) faced by Mozambican households in **Xai-Xai District** and documented **coping strategies** to inform resilience-building interventions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65984).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS)** facilitated **crop improvement** by tracking adoption of **improved bean varieties** in Mozambique, linking variety uptake to **income growth and climate variability resilience** for rural households (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MOCXUN).

• **CGIAR (IRI, CCAFS)** scaled **agricultural index insurance tools**, enabling **data-driven risk management** for over **one million households** across Africa, including Mozambique (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121437).

• **CGIAR (CIP)** introduced the **Sweetpotato Triple S technology** in **Manica Province** to address **prolonged droughts and extreme weather**, validating five drought-resistant varieties for smallholder farmers (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/FM3IMX).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS, AGNES)** conducted **capacity-building workshops** on **objective seasonal forecasting** and **climate services co-production**, strengthening institutional linkages for **agriculture and food security** in Eastern/Southern Africa (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941).

• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **climate vulnerability and resilience** in Mozambique, highlighting **economic impacts of cyclones, flooding, and rainfall variability** on **maize production, hydropower, and infrastructure** (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136961).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS)** modeled **maize yield reductions under climate change** (2050/2080) and proposed **adaptation measures** for **Southern Africa’s maize mega-environments**, including Mozambique (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

• **CGIAR (AGNES)** strengthened **African climate negotiators’ capacity** in **attribution science**, linking **greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes** to inform **adaptation-mitigation dialogues** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).
Mozambique.mitigation:
• **CGIAR (AGNES)** focused on **attribution science** to build evidence for **greenhouse gas emission impacts** on climate extremes, supporting **African negotiators** in **international climate policy discussions** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).

• **No direct mitigation interventions** (e.g., carbon sequestration, low-emission agriculture) were identified in this sample for Mozambique.
Mozambique.water:
• **CGIAR researchers** assessed **climate change impacts on water resources**, including **flooding risks in Zambezia/Nampula Provinces**, **Zambezi River hydropower vulnerabilities**, and **irrigation needs** under **increased rainfall variability** (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136961).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS)** analyzed **biophysical impacts of climate change on maize production**, indirectly addressing **water-use efficiency** under **nitrogen-level scenarios** (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).

Zimbabwe.narrative: CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience, food security, and conflict-sensitive adaptation** in **Zimbabwe** through evidence-based policy frameworks, capacity-building workshops, and targeted research on vulnerable populations. Specifically:
Zimbabwe.adaptation:
• **CGIAR Climate Security Team** developed a **common vision framework** linking climate impacts to peace and security risks, addressing resource competition (e.g., water scarcity) and gendered vulnerabilities while recommending conflict-sensitive climate finance and Indigenous knowledge integration (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/172813).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS (Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security)** advanced **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) measurement, reporting, and verification (MRV)** to align agricultural development with climate goals, though gaps in coordinated tracking of CSA projects were identified (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99462).

• **CGIAR’s AGNES (African Group of Negotiators Expert Support)** strengthened **attribution science capacity** for African climate negotiators, linking greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes to inform negotiations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).

• **CGIAR (via ILRI & IRI for Climate and Society)** hosted a **workshop in Victoria Falls** to enhance **climate services for smallholder farmers**, addressing constraints in producing and communicating farmer-relevant climate information (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68986).

• **CGIAR’s FOCUS Climate Security Observatory** identified **hotspots of climate-induced conflict** (e.g., Beitbridge, Chipinge districts) where harsh climate interacts with low productivity and undernutrition, mapping vulnerabilities for policy integration (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116461).

• **CGIAR (CR4D initiative)** conducted a **capacity-building workshop** in Victoria Falls to improve **objective seasonal forecasting** for agriculture and food security, fostering South-South partnerships among 15 African nations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941).

• **CGIAR highlighted disabilities as a climate vulnerability** in early warning systems, emphasizing barriers to access for persons with disabilities during disasters (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/177394).

• **CGIAR (via Bioversity/CGIAR Research Program on Agriculture for Nutrition and Health)** analyzed **land-use shifts** in the Mid Zambezi Valley, demonstrating how declining cotton profitability led to wildlife habitat loss and increased human-wildlife conflicts, proposing conservation strategies for multifunctional landscapes (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VMVZRD).
Zimbabwe.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s AGNES** advanced **attribution science** to quantify the role of industrialized vs. developing regions in driving climate change, supporting evidence-based mitigation strategies for African negotiators (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).

Cameroon.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Cameroon** through **value chain innovations, gender-sensitive policies, and climate-smart technologies**. Specifically:
- **CIAT/CGIAR** assessed **climate vulnerability in cocoa and potato systems** via participatory research and adaptation strategies (e.g., shade trees, erosion control) (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/dvn/5w7lgw](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/5w7lgw), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/5W7LGW](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/5W7LGW)).
- **CGIAR’s Green Innovation Centres** analyzed **climate risks in cocoa, potato, and poultry value chains** to inform adaptation investments (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111277](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111277)).
- **AICCRA/CGIAR** scaled **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) technologies** through regional partnerships, including training modules for universities in Cameroon (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137738](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137738)).
Cameroon.adaptation:
• **CGIAR (CIAT, World Agroforestry Centre)** identified **shade trees and agroforestry** as critical adaptation strategies for cocoa in Cameroon’s southern regions, where climate vulnerability is lowest, contrasting with high-risk forest-savanna transitions in neighboring countries (Sources: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/dvn/5w7lgw](https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/5w7lgw), [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/5W7LGW](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/5W7LGW)).

• **CGIAR’s Green Innovation Centres** mapped **climate hazards and adaptation opportunities** in Cameroon’s potato, cocoa, and poultry value chains, recommending innovations like early-maturing crops and pest-resistant seeds to manage risks (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111277](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111277)).

• **CGIAR (AICCRA)** documented **gender-disparate coping strategies** in Cameroon’s Southwest, highlighting women’s reliance on livelihood diversification and men’s migration, informing gender-sensitive climate policies (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/83068](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/83068)).

• **CGIAR (CIP/IITA)** proposed **regional adaptation zones** for cocoa in West Africa, classifying Cameroon’s southern areas as low-vulnerability zones suitable for intensification, while high-risk zones require crop diversification (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-016-9707-y](https://doi.org/10.1007/s11027-016-9707-y)).

• **CGIAR (CIAT)** evaluated **forage adaptation** in mixed crop-livestock systems, demonstrating erosion control via hedgerows and intercropping with legumes (e.g., *Canavalia*, *Desmodium*) to address land scarcity (Source: [https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HBO4EC](https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HBO4EC)).

• **CGIAR (AICCRA/RUFORUM)** accelerated **scaling of CSA/CIS technologies** through university partnerships in Cameroon, integrating climate-resilient practices into curricula (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137738](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137738)).
Cameroon.mitigation: • **CGIAR (World Agroforestry Centre)** framed **afforestation, reforestation, and REDD+** as mitigation priorities in Cameroon’s tropical ecosystems, emphasizing research gaps in ecological and socioeconomic baseline data (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/20317](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/20317)).

Costa Rica.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA), resilience, and mitigation strategies** in **Costa Rica** through participatory scenario planning, digital innovation, and sector-specific interventions. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) Program** developed **future scenario methodologies** to enhance Costa Rica’s Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) and strengthen climate resilience (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115529).
- **Bioversity International and CIAT** piloted **digital emergency response systems** to restore agricultural infrastructure and improve climate resilience after extreme events (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/102482).
Costa Rica.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** proposed **methods to measure climate change impacts on food and nutrition security** in Central America, including Costa Rica (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80948).
• **Bioversity International** implemented a **crop diversity strategy** to strengthen farmers’ adaptive capacity to climate change, combining climate modeling and participatory research (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** facilitated **participatory scenario planning** to enhance Costa Rica’s 2020 NDC, increasing resilience and emission reduction ambitions (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121243).
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and Bioversity** supported the **digitalization of extension data** for drought risk management, enabling mobile-based vulnerability assessments (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121254).
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** contributed to **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) scaling** by fostering enabling institutional environments, including national policy alignment in Costa Rica (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/77226).
Costa Rica.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS Program** analyzed **challenges and opportunities for Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions (NAMAs) in the livestock sector**, focusing on GHG reduction through market-driven transformations in Costa Rica (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/81300).
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** promoted **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) as a mitigation strategy**, reducing agriculture’s greenhouse gas burden while improving productivity (Source_https://hdl.handle.net/10568/77226).

Benin.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA), adaptive resilience, and climate finance mobilization** in **Benin** through participatory research, capacity building, and policy-relevant assessments. Specifically:
- **CIAT, Bioversity International, and IWMI** evaluated **land-water management practices** to enhance farmer resilience (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).
- **AICCRA and the iGST WA Hub** trained **West African CSOs** to access climate finance (e.g., GCF) for locally relevant adaptation and mitigation (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563).
Benin.adaptation:
• **CIAT, Bioversity International, and national partners** piloted a **seed resilience strategy** in Benin, combining participatory methods and crop modeling to help farmers access climate-adapted maize varieties (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/96090, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).

• **CCAFS and CORAF** assessed the **climate-smartness of WAAPP projects** in Benin, strengthening stakeholder capacity to mainstream CSA (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455).

• **AIC-BOAD (Alliance Bioversity-CIAT)** developed a **training manual for participatory, village-based CSA projects**, integrating field visits to demonstrate best practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/144087).

• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **gender-responsive climate services** in Benin, identifying barriers to women’s access to climate information and proposing ICT-based solutions (https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216).

• **CIAT and IWMI** conducted a **household survey** on integrated land-water management for climate adaptation, identifying scalable practices (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

• **CGIAR** published a **Benin CSA profile** to guide investments in climate-smart agricultural practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97615).
Benin.mitigation: • **AICCRA** supported **climate finance mobilization** by training CSOs in Benin to develop **bankable GCF concept notes** for locally relevant mitigation actions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563).
Benin.water: • **CIAT, IWMI, and IFPRI** evaluated **land-water management projects** in Benin to enhance farmer resilience to climate variability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

Israel.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Israel** focused exclusively on **water-related disease modeling** through the **LATEBLIGHT model**, a tool for simulating *Phytophthora infestans* epidemics on potato foliage. Specifically:
- The Center validated and expanded the **LATEBLIGHT model’s applicability** across Israeli locations (Mivtakhim, Nir-Eliyahu, Nir-Oz, Elbaz) by comparing model predictions to observed late blight epidemics in 13 potato cultivars (2000–2001), confirming its accuracy for 8 cultivars while identifying inconsistencies in host resistance estimates (Sources: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/GTV7Y4**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/XD4APA**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/XIF8Q9**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/XKKVJS**).
Israel.water:
• CGIAR **validated and extended the LATEBLIGHT model’s domain** for Israel, demonstrating its sensitivity to **weather variables** (e.g., initial inoculum, temperature) over host resistance in predicting *Phytophthora infestans* outbreaks across multiple potato-growing regions (Mivtakhim, Nir-Eliyahu, Nir-Oz, Elbaz) (Sources: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/GTV7Y4**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/XD4APA**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/XIF8Q9**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/XKKVJS**).

• The Center **assessed model performance** for 13 potato cultivars in Israel (2000–2001), achieving 8/13 accurate predictions while highlighting gaps in host resistance data as a limitation (Sources: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/GTV7Y4**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21223/P3/XD4APA**).

Egypt.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Egypt** through evidence-based policy advocacy, technological suites, and capacity-building. Specifically:
- The Center advanced **climate-adaptive agricultural strategies** via modeling and technology suites to counteract productivity declines (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321)).
- Researchers assessed **gender-responsive climate services** to address disparities in access to rural climate information (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).
Egypt.adaptation:
• CGIAR **modeled climate change impacts on Egypt’s agriculture** and proposed a ‘suite of technologies’ (e.g., crop traits, water management) to mitigate yield declines for crops like wheat, rice, and fruits/vegetables, though no single solution fully offsets losses for maize, sugar, and oilseeds (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321) and [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179790](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179790) for MENA nexus work).

• The Center **developed climate-yield models** for PepsiCo’s potato supply chain, enabling data-driven adaptation practices via the **Climate Resilience Platform** to enhance resilience across 12 countries, including Egypt (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422)).

• Researchers **analyzed gender disparities in climate service access**, recommending ICT-based solutions and women’s group inclusion to improve rural adaptation (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216](https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216)).

• CGIAR **published climate-resilience policies** for Egypt’s agriculture, emphasizing sustainable productivity and food security amid climate threats (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896294189](https://doi.org/10.2499/9780896294189) and [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/134159](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/134159)).

• The **FOCUS Climate Security team** trained **35+ water engineers** in **conflict-sensitive water management** to foster climate-resilient peace, addressing tensions exacerbated by water scarcity (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174314](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174314)).
Egypt.mitigation: • CGIAR **advocated for global cooperation on greenhouse gas mitigation**, highlighting Egypt’s vulnerability to climate-induced food market disruptions and the societal costs of inaction (Sources: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134318](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134318) and [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179790](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179790)).
Egypt.water:
• CGIAR **integrated water management** into climate adaptation strategies, recommending shifts toward importing high-water-consuming cereals (e.g., maize) under future arid conditions to preserve domestic productivity (Source: [https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321](https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.134321)).

• The Center **trained water engineers** in **conflict-sensitive frameworks** to align sustainable water arrangements with climate-resilient peacebuilding (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174314](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174314)).

Sudan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and socio-economic adaptation** in **Sudan** through multi-disciplinary research and capacity-building initiatives. Specifically:
- The Center analyzed **climate-induced migration risks** in East Africa, linking droughts to youth outmigration and socio-economic vulnerabilities (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278**).
- Researchers modeled **water demand, crop yield variability, and economic impacts** under climate change, projecting extreme negative variability costs up to **$109.5 billion** by 2050 (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101350**).
Sudan.adaptation:
• CGIAR assessed **climate change impacts on agricultural production**, highlighting increased frequency and severity of extreme events as key risks for farmers in Sudan and East Africa (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106796**).

• The Center **co-produced climate information services** through a regional workshop in Zimbabwe, strengthening institutional linkages for **seasonal forecasting and food security** in Sudan and 14 other African nations (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941**).

• Researchers mapped **climate-conflict hotspots** in Sudan, identifying socio-economic vulnerabilities (e.g., undernutrition, low productivity) as exacerbating factors for insecurity in regions like **Marsabit Central and Wajir East** (Sources: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116463**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116462**).

• CGIAR contributed to the **East and Horn of Africa Climate Action Plan (2023–2028)**, aligning adaptation measures for displaced populations with regional frameworks like **IGAD’s Climate Strategy** (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370**).

• The Center supported **livestock and pastoralism adaptation** in the IGAD region, fostering research-for-development partnerships to address climate vulnerabilities (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053**).

• A social media analysis revealed **policy gaps** in Sudan, where climate, conflict, and socio-economic insecurities remain disassociated in public communications (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116453**).
Sudan.water:
• CGIAR integrated **hydrological and crop models** (e.g., IMPACT, CGE) to simulate **water stress and yield variability** under climate change, quantifying economic losses from extreme droughts in Sudan (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101350**).

• Researchers examined **maize production vulnerabilities** in sub-Saharan Africa, including Sudan’s **maize mega-environments (MMEs)**, and modeled yield reductions under climate change scenarios (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005**).

Thailand.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and resilience-building** in **Thailand** through **policy-linked mitigation, adaptation frameworks, and regional collaboration**. Specifically:
- **CCAFS-SEA** facilitated **climate-smart village (CSV) criteria and regional strategies** to align R4D efforts across CGIAR centers (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34865**).
- **IRRI-CCAFS** designed **low-emission rice production systems** and **MRV tools** to quantify GHG reductions for Thailand’s NDC targets (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121427**).
Thailand.adaptation:
• **CCAFS-SEA** convened **regional workshops** to upscale **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) options**, including climate information services and insurance mechanisms (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/90537**).
• **CCAFS-SEA** organized **symposia** to exchange **adaptation practices** for smallholder farmers, including decision support systems for climate risks (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003**).
• **CGIAR centers (IRRI, CIAT, ICRISAT, etc.)** drafted a **regional strategy** for climate-resilient agriculture, including **climate-smart village (CSV) selection criteria** (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34865**).
• **CCAFS-SEA** hosted **annual coordination meetings** to track progress in **CSV sites** and **climate-resilient food security** projects (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106894**).
• **CGIAR-R4D** collaborated with **ASEAN CRN** to strengthen **climate resilience research** in Southeast Asia, including Thailand (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101149**).
Thailand.mitigation:
• **CCAFS-SEA** contributed to **Thailand’s Rice NAMA** by developing **GHG measurement tools (SECTOR)** and scaling low-emission rice practices to **25,000+ households** (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121427**).
• **NAMA Facility** funded a **€15M project** to support **mitigation actions in Thailand’s rice sector**, leveraging private-sector investments (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106302**).
• **CCAFS-SEA researchers** presented **mitigation options for agricultural GHG emissions** in regional symposia (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107003**).

Burundi.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and food security** in **Burundi** through targeted research on crop improvement, climate-smart technologies, and vulnerability assessments. Specifically:
- **CIAT** led studies on **improved bean varieties** to enhance yields and resilience (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MOCXUN*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UMR2OC*).
- **CCAFS** and partners analyzed **climate hazards** and **adaptation strategies** for smallholder farmers (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/27955*).
- **CGIAR Centers** explored **gender gaps in climate-smart adoption** and **soil carbon sequestration** as mitigation pathways (Sources: *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/KQJUEP*, *https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/3BLW7E*).
Burundi.adaptation:
• **CIAT-PABRA** assessed the **impact of improved bean varieties** on smallholder productivity, food security, and resilience to agro-climatic shocks in Burundi, using household-level data and DNA fingerprinting (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UMR2OC).
• **CGIAR/NARS** documented **bean variety adoption patterns** by location, adopter, and source, linking crop improvement to income growth for rural households (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MOCXUN).
• **CCAFS** explored **climate risk profiles** for Burundi, identifying precipitation and temperature hazards to inform adaptation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0).
• **CGIAR** facilitated **capacity-building workshops** on objective climate forecasts for agriculture and food security, strengthening institutional linkages in Eastern and Southern Africa (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941).
• **CGIAR Centers** examined **gender dynamics in climate-smart agricultural (CSA) adoption**, highlighting gaps in access and use among smallholder bean farmers (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/KQJUEP).
• **CCAFS** analyzed **vulnerability to future climate change** in East Africa, including Burundi, through scenario-based policy planning (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/27955).
• **CGIAR** contributed to **climate-smart maize adaptation** research, modeling biophysical impacts on production and food security under future climate scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
Burundi.mitigation:
• **CGIAR** conducted a **systematic review of soil organic carbon (SOC) responses** to agronomic best management practices (BMPs) in East Africa, including Burundi, to quantify SOC sequestration potential (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/3BLW7E).
• **CGIAR** analyzed **land cover/land use change (LCLUC) and greenhouse gas (GHG) impacts** on regional climate dynamics, emphasizing mitigation strategies for food production systems (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-011-0116-7).

Congo.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, resilience, and institutional capacity-building** in the **Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC)** through participatory research, curriculum integration, and policy-informed interventions. Specifically:
- **CIAT** assessed on-farm niches for improved forages to address livestock feed constraints in Sud-Kivu (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HBO4EC).
- **AICCRA (via RUFORUM/WASCAL)** mainstreamed Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) and Climate Information Services (CIS) modules in DRC universities to enhance climate-resilient agricultural education (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179613).
Congo.adaptation:
• **CIAT** conducted participatory on-farm research in Sud-Kivu to evaluate forage legumes (e.g., *Canavalia brasiliensis*, *Desmodium uncinatum*) and grasses (e.g., Napier grass) for erosion control, land scarcity, and mixed crop-livestock systems, identifying farmer preferences and agro-ecological constraints (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HBO4EC).

• **AGNES (CGIAR-affiliated)** strengthened African climate negotiators’ capacity in attribution science to link greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes, supporting DRC’s role in global climate policy (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).

• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed climate vulnerability in Bukavu, documenting adaptation strategies (e.g., erosion hedgerows, intercropping) and barriers (e.g., land tenure insecurity) to inform policy integration (https://doi.org/10.1177/1070496514536395).

• **CGIAR** integrated CSA modules into DRC universities, fostering faculty capacity and student engagement in climate-resilient agricultural practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179613).

• **CGIAR** assessed institutional adaptive capacity in the Congo Basin (Cameroon/DRC border regions), highlighting weak intergovernmental linkages as a constraint to resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/20394).
Congo.mitigation:

• **CGIAR** contributed to attribution science training for African negotiators, equipping DRC stakeholders to advocate for mitigation responsibilities in COP negotiations (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).

Somalia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate adaptation, mitigation, and security linkages** in **Somalia** through policy analysis, capacity-building, and interdisciplinary research. Specifically:
- **Bioversity/CIAT** facilitated workshops to integrate climate, peace, and security frameworks (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/169331**).
- **CGIAR-affiliated researchers** analyzed climate-migration nexus and youth vulnerability (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278**).
Somalia.mitigation:
