Sri Lanka.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient water governance and agricultural adaptation** in Sri Lanka through evidence-based policy influence, sector-specific vulnerability assessments, and scalable climate-smart interventions. Specifically:
- **IWMI** analyzed water governance challenges and policy options for climate adaptation across irrigation, fisheries, and non-agricultural sectors (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-032-04950-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-032-04950-6)).
- **CCAFS** advanced Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) through upscaling workshops, climate information services, and insurance models (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/90537](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/90537)).
Sri Lanka.adaptation:
• **IWMI** developed methodologies for sector vulnerability profiles, shaping Sri Lanka’s **National Climate Change Adaptation Strategy (2011–2016)** and **Second National Communication to UNFCCC**, while driving demand for climate stations and stakeholder capacity building (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67169](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67169)).

• **IWMI** influenced climate-resilient household food security programs by integrating research on water-agriculture impacts into UNDP initiatives (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/32778](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/32778)).

• **CCAFS** facilitated regional meetings to scale climate-smart agricultural practices, emphasizing policy models and big-data analytics for South Asia (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106626](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106626)).

• **IWMI** synthesized water-related climate risks, proposing transformative adaptation frameworks for water, food, and livelihood security (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-032-04950-6_1](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-032-04950-6_1)).

• **IWMI** assessed farmer perceptions of climate/land-use pressures in dry-zone tank systems, informing localized adaptation strategies (Source: [https://doi.org/10.3390/su16198603](https://doi.org/10.3390/su16198603)).
Sri Lanka.water:
• **IWMI** analyzed climate projections for Sri Lanka, highlighting **increased water stress, erratic precipitation, and regional flood/drought risks** (e.g., Northern vs. Western provinces) (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-032-04950-6_5](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-032-04950-6_5)).

• **IWMI** examined water governance challenges across **major/minor irrigation, fisheries, and domestic sectors**, proposing policy reforms for resilience (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-032-04950-6](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-032-04950-6)).

• **IWMI** projected **7% increase in mean annual rainfall by 2050**, with shifts in agro-ecological zones (e.g., dry-zone areas transitioning to intermediate zones) (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67629](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67629)).

Guinea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and resilience-building** in **Guinea** through stakeholder capacity strengthening, climate risk profiling, and integrated mitigation-adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- **CCAFS West Africa** assessed climate-smartness of WAAPP projects to enhance stakeholder capacity in mainstreaming CSA (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455**).
- **CGIAR-affiliated researchers** developed climate risk profiles for Guinea, mapping precipitation and temperature hazards to inform adaptation planning (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0**).
Guinea.adaptation:
• **CCAFS West Africa** led participatory assessments of the **West Africa Agricultural Productivity Programme (WAAPP)** in Guinea, strengthening stakeholder capacity to mainstream **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA)** and integrate climate information for enhanced resilience (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455**).

• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **climate change impacts on maize production** in Guinea (as part of sub-Saharan Africa), identifying yield reductions under future scenarios (2050/2080) and proposing adaptation measures for improved varieties and input management (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005**).

• **CGIAR’s CaSCIERA-TA project** expanded CSA tools and knowledge in Guinea, selecting climate-informed agricultural practices based on **past, prevailing, and projected climate data** (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121338**).

• **CGIAR-affiliated studies** reviewed **adaptation strategies** for West African agriculture (including Guinea), synthesizing sector-specific (crop/livestock/fishery) policies and institutional mechanisms to improve farming resilience (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3**).
Guinea.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s BIODEV Programme** demonstrated **biocarbon approaches** in Guinea to link **climate change mitigation and adaptation**, aiming to reduce greenhouse gas emissions while enhancing rural livelihoods and institutional capacity (Sources: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/ZDW61D**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/K44TXF**).

Germany.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, adaptation strategies, and mitigation research** in **Germany** through policy-relevant presentations, stakeholder engagement, and cross-institutional collaborations. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** hosted high-impact conferences in **Berlin and Hamburg** to bridge research and policy on agricultural emissions and adaptation (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99574, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99575).
- **IFPRI researchers** critiqued reductive interpretations of **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA)** to emphasize its dual role in **adaptation and mitigation** (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99575).
- **CGIAR’s Challenge Program on Water and Food** (via partnerships with the **University of Hamburg** and African institutions) supported **policy tools for climate adaptation** in Africa, funded by Germany’s **Federal Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development** (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154495).
Germany.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** presented **climate risks to the coffee sector** (Arabica coffee) and adaptation strategies at the **Kaffeeröster-Tagung in Hamburg**, targeting industry stakeholders (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79869).
• **IFPRI (via CGIAR)** analyzed **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA)** frameworks, highlighting how narrow interpretations limit its adaptive and food-security benefits (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99575).
• **CGIAR’s Challenge Program on Water and Food** (collaborating with **Ethiopian and South African partners**) developed **policy tools to assess climate vulnerability** and enhance adaptive capacity in rural Africa, with German funding (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154495).
Germany.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS and CIAT** led a **keynote presentation on agricultural mitigation costs and implementation** at the **International Conference on Agricultural Emissions (Berlin, 2018)**, addressing policy gaps (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99574).
• **CGIAR (via CAAS-IEDA, China Agricultural University, and Wageningen University)** launched a **project to pilot low-emission dairy farming in China**, with German-linked CGIAR support, focusing on **GHG and nitrogen reduction** in large-scale systems (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111194, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110569).

Netherlands.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, resilient agribusiness, and low-emission development** in the **Netherlands** through partnerships with Dutch ministries, private sector actors, and research institutions. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** designed a **SME self-assessment tool** to accelerate sustainable finance readiness in agriculture (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114725).
- **CCAFS** catalyzed **€34 million in private-sector co-investment** for climate-resilient agribusiness in East Africa, leveraging Dutch government funding (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121858).
Netherlands.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Bioversity International (via CCAFS)** enhanced local community access to **crop genetic diversity for climate adaptation**, supporting participatory methods in Benin (with Dutch policy alignment) to identify climate-resilient maize traits (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/96090).
• **CCAFS** developed a **climate-informed business case framework** (CRAFT project) to support resilient agribusiness, funded by the Dutch government (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121858).
• **CCAFS** facilitated a **sustainable finance self-assessment tool** for SMEs, addressing systemic barriers with input from the Dutch Ministry of Nature, Agriculture, and Food Quality (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/114725).
Netherlands.mitigation:
• **CGIAR (via CCAFS/Wageningen UR collaboration)** researched **GHG mitigation strategies in dairy sectors**, including carbon footprint assessments and low-emission development options for large-scale farms in China (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111194, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99446, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110576).
• **CCAFS** influenced Dutch policy by demonstrating **food loss and waste (FLW) as a critical GHG contributor**, leading to the Dutch Ministry’s **‘Food Systems Approach: FLW and GHGe’s’** initiative (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121257, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/124759).
• **CCAFS** supported the Dutch **‘Taskforce Circular Economy in Food’**, integrating research on **FLW reduction and GHG emissions** to stimulate circular innovations (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121256).

Pakistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and water-food-economy linkages** in **Pakistan** through integrated modeling, farmer capacity building, and climate risk profiling. Specifically:
- **IFPRI** developed a **Social Accounting Matrix (SAM) with hydrological integration** to model water-agriculture-economy interactions (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2METW0).
- **Bioversity International and CIAT** scaled **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) profiles** across 13 districts, training 1,000+ farmers and aligning with provincial policies (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121222).
- **PepsiCo (via CGIAR-linked Climate Resilience Platform)** modeled climate risks for potato supply chains, enabling adaptation prioritization (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422).
Pakistan.adaptation:
• **IFPRI** assessed **climate change impacts on rice/wheat** in the Indo-Gangetic Plains, projecting yield reductions (6–25%) by 2080 under high-emission scenarios, with region-specific adaptation needs (Source: https://doi.org/10.54386/jam.v16i1.1480).
• **World Food Program (via CGIAR-linked data)** profiled **climate hazards** (temperature/precipitation) for Pakistan to inform risk-based adaptation (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0).
• **Bioversity/CIAT** designed **Farmer Field School curricula** in Sindh/KPK using CSA profiles, endorsed by provincial governments (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121222).
• **CGIAR researchers** demonstrated **adaptation gains** (e.g., timely sowing, irrigation efficiency) could offset wheat yield losses in India/Pakistan’s agro-climates (Source: https://doi.org/10.3354/cr01212).
• **CGIAR** highlighted **hybrid adaptive knowledge** (local + scientific) as critical for climate resilience in Asian Highlands, including Pakistan (Source: https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7).
Pakistan.mitigation: • **CGIAR** quantified **agricultural GHG emissions** (37% of global total) in Asia-Pacific, emphasizing mitigation-adaptation synergies (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/159403).
Pakistan.water:
• **IFPRI/IWMI** integrated **hydrological data** (tube-well census, surface water flows) into Pakistan’s **Social Accounting Matrix** to model water-food-economy trade-offs (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2METW0).
• **IFPRI** conducted the **Pakistan Rural Household Panel Survey (PRHPS)** to track **agricultural water use** across Punjab, Sindh, and KPK (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/T9GGYA).
• **CGIAR** analyzed **adaptation strategies** for water scarcity (e.g., irrigation efficiency) in South Asia’s climate-vulnerable regions (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67576).

Australia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience, mitigation, and adaptation** in **Australia** through targeted research on agricultural systems, pastoral livelihoods, and policy integration. Specifically:
- The Center advanced **climate risk management frameworks** for semi-arid farming systems, linking Australian case studies to global smallholder applications (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/105813**).
- Researchers developed **system dynamics models** to assess drought impacts on rangelands and inform adaptive grazing strategies for pastoralists (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14669**).
- CGIAR supported **multi-year climate and food security plans** for APEC economies, including Australia, through stakeholder consultations and policy alignment (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197**).
Australia.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed **climate-yield models** for supply-chain resilience, integrating PepsiCo’s potato program data into actionable adaptation practices via the **Climate Resilience Platform** (applied globally, including Australia’s semi-arid contexts) (**https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422**).

• Researchers at **ILRI** (via CGIAR collaboration) analyzed **climate variability impacts on rangelands**, using system dynamics to quantify drought recovery times and recommend farming strategies to enhance pastoralist adaptability in semi-arid Australian systems (**https://doi.org/10.1111/gcb.14669**).

• The Center documented **Indigenous perspectives on climate adaptation** in NE Arnhem Land, designing community-led models to strengthen adaptive capacity by addressing poverty and cultural self-sufficiency (**https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2010.05.002**).

• CGIAR’s **Commission on Sustainable Agriculture and Climate Change** (led by Dr. Megan Clark) assessed **climate-induced food production risks** in Australia, framing policy priorities for regional vibrancy (**https://hdl.handle.net/10568/10486**).

• The Center provided **strategic support to APEC** (2017–2021) to develop a **Multi-Year Action Plan on Climate Change and Food Security**, including Australian stakeholder consultations (**https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197**).
Australia.mitigation:
• CGIAR synthesized **land-based mitigation opportunities** in agriculture, highlighting Australia’s role in reducing greenhouse gases through farming system changes (e.g., livestock, agroforestry) and cross-country lessons for smallholders (**https://hdl.handle.net/10568/16715**).

• Researchers explored **sustainable use of natural resources** in food supply systems, addressing climate change impacts at the **Australasian Agricultural and Resource Economics Society (AARES) Annual Conference** (**https://hdl.handle.net/10568/133626**).

Madagascar.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive capacity** in **Madagascar** through **crop diversity, policy tools, and genetic resource strategies**. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** developed a **seed resilience strategy** to enhance farmers’ access to climate-adapted crop varieties (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).
- **CCAFS** supported **national policy development** for implementing the **Plant Treaty and Nagoya Protocol**, enabling access to adapted genetic materials for climate-stressed agricultural systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121359, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121361).
Madagascar.adaptation:
• **Bioversity International** piloted a **seed resilience strategy** in Madagascar, combining climate modeling and participatory methods to strengthen farmers’ access to diverse, climate-adapted crop varieties (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).
• **CCAFS** provided **decision-making tools** to national authorities, enabling the adoption of **2018 guidelines** for implementing laws on **plant genetic resources**—critical for climate-stressed local production systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121359).
• **CCAFS** demonstrated the **utility of global genetic resource-sharing systems** to policymakers via **local climate vulnerability analyses**, informing national policies for climate resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123219).
• **CCAFS** contributed to **new national laws** in Madagascar (and Benin) to **access and utilize adapted genetic materials** under the **ITPGRFA**, addressing crops negatively affected by climate change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121361).
Madagascar.mitigation: • **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **multifunctional agroforestry landscapes** in Madagascar (and other tropics), showing how **agroforestry systems** (e.g., rubber-integrated landscapes) can **mitigate deforestation** while supporting biodiversity and livelihoods (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/25199).

Morocco.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture, adaptation, and policy integration** in **Morocco** through **investment planning, youth engagement, and resilience-building innovations**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** designed **Climate-Smart Investment Plans (CSAIPs) indicators** for Morocco, focusing on **conservation agriculture** (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/KO3LT9).
- **CCAFS and partners** facilitated **youth engagement in climate-smart agriculture** through discussions and events culminating at **COP22 in Marrakech** (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92979).
- **ICARDA (International Center for Agricultural Research in Dry Areas)** enhanced **agro-ecosystem resilience** via **pollinator-based farming** and **climate adaptation surveys** across Moroccan regions (Sources: hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/RT7QQ6, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/HTYFWV).

• **CGIAR/IFPRI** informed **policymaking** on **climate change and hunger** via **Global Landscapes Forum sessions in Marrakech** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/133334, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/133332).
Morocco.mitigation:

• **CGIAR/CLIFF-GRADS** facilitated **international collaboration** on **greenhouse gas research**, including Moroccan participation via **Mohammed VI Polytechnic University** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110095).

Sierra Leone.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and sustainable agriculture** in **Sierra Leone** through **integrated land-water management, regional adaptation planning, and biocarbon-based mitigation strategies**. Specifically:

Panama.narrative:

- **CGIAR-CCAFS** assessed climate suitability for cocoa, coffee, and agroforestry trees using **Species Distribution Modeling (SDM)** to inform adaptation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/0O1GW1).
- **CGIAR-CCAFS** led the **SICA CSA regional strategy (2018–2030)**, a robust policy framework for climate adaptation in Central America (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121429).
Panama.adaptation:
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** evaluated climate suitability for **cocoa, coffee, and agroforestry trees** in Mesoamerica (Panama to Mexico) using **ensemble Species Distribution Modeling (SDM)** to identify vulnerable species and inform abiotic stress mitigation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/0O1GW1).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** facilitated **Mesas Técnicas Agroclimáticas (MTA)**, multi-stakeholder dialogues to **understand climate variability risks** and generate localized recommendations for farmers in Latin America (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/108780).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** scaled a **Climate Risk Management (CRM) strategy** across **11 Latin American countries**, reaching **500,000+ farmers** with climate services for resilient decision-making (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121217).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** evaluated the **Sustainable Climate-Adapted Agriculture Strategy (EASAC)** in the **SICA region**, synthesizing results for policy alignment in Panama and Central America (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119642).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** developed a **future scenarios methodology** to design a **plausible-pathway-resilient CSA strategy** for SICA, attracting **$2M in climate adaptation investments** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121429).

• **CGIAR-CCAFS** studied **biological corridors (BC) for plant dispersal** in fragmented Mesoamerican landscapes, using **spatial modeling** to assess their role in **biodiversity adaptation under climate change** (https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.672).

• **CGIAR** conducted **transcriptomic analysis** of banana varieties to identify **disease-resistant genes (Fusarium oxysporum TR4)**, comparing resistant (‘Pahang’) and susceptible (‘Brazilian Cavendish’) corms (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/W2M6WG).
Panama.mitigation: • **CGIAR-CCAFS** advised the **Central American Agricultural Council (CAC)** on **greenhouse gas (GHG) accounting** and **mitigation practices** in the agricultural sector, providing training to regional decision-makers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/124664).

Solomon Islands.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation** in **Solomon Islands** through participatory community-based assessments and economic modeling of climate impacts. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** developed a **four-step participatory action research framework** to guide coastal communities in assessing and planning climate adaptation, including scoping climate-sensitive livelihoods, evaluating adaptation options, and implementing action plans (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69488, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69495, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69490, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69494, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69489, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69493).
- **WorldFish (CGIAR Research Program on Fish Agri-Food Systems)** modeled the **economic potential of climate adaptation strategies** (e.g., fish aggregating devices, aquaculture, and natural resource management) to mitigate per capita fish consumption decline due to climate change (Source: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004).
Solomon Islands.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS program** designed a **community-based adaptation assessment toolkit** for Solomon Islands, including **Landscape Function Analysis** to evaluate home garden production systems and **Ecosystem Services Mapping** to identify critical natural resources for resilient fishing and farming (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69495, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69494).

• **CGIAR** facilitated **participatory workshops** (e.g., Impact and Adaptation Assessment Workshops) to co-develop climate-sensitive farming and fishing practices, focusing on community-led decision-making for adaptation (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69488, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69490, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69489).

• **WorldFish (CGIAR)** assessed **climate vulnerability in Solomon Islands**, highlighting limited adaptive capacity due to high dependence on agriculture (68% employment) and GDP contribution (39%), and proposed **low-cost inshore FADs** as a climate adaptation strategy to augment fish supplies (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/145066, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2016.01.004).

• **CGIAR** documented **implementation planning workshops** to guide communities in selecting and executing adaptation actions, addressing social, economic, and environmental thresholds (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69493).

Canada.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and food security** in **Canada** through strategic policy support and adaptation research. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s CCAFS (Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security)** facilitated stakeholder consultations to develop a **Multi-Year Action Plan on Climate Change and Food Security** for APEC economies, including Canada (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197).
- **CGIAR researchers** analyzed climate-induced shocks (e.g., droughts, floods) to propose **adaptation measures** for vulnerable communities, with case studies extending to global relevance (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/147151).
Canada.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Resilience Platform** (developed via PepsiCo’s potato program) enabled **supply-chain resilience modeling** to identify climate risks and prioritize adaptation practices across 12 countries, including Canada’s agricultural systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422).
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** supported **APEC economies** in designing a **Multi-Year Plan on Climate Change and Food Security (2018–2021)**, incorporating stakeholder-driven priorities for climate adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197).
• **CGIAR researchers** investigated **drought-induced crop failure** and food insecurity, proposing resilience strategies for vulnerable regions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/147151).
Canada.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s agricultural research** contributed to global syntheses on **land-based climate change mitigation**, highlighting Canada’s experiences in reducing greenhouse gas emissions through farming systems (e.g., livestock, crops, agroforestry) as models for smallholder adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/16715).

Tunisia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart adaptation and sustainable land management in Tunisia** through targeted research on rangeland restoration, value chain resilience, and bioeconomic modeling. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program** analyzed climate vulnerabilities in potato and dairy value chains to inform adaptation priorities (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111410**).
- **Bioversity International and ICARDA researchers** assessed rangeland degradation resilience, testing rest periods and land tenure systems to rehabilitate degraded rangelands in southern Tunisia (Sources: **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/2ZQCH3**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/QBZTMV**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/ZC9K8J**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/ZAJDIV**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/X9IYIU**, **hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/IKPUUB**).
Tunisia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** conducted a climate vulnerability analysis of potato and dairy value chains in Northwest and Central West Tunisia, identifying adaptation priorities and recommending innovations to manage climate risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111410).

• **CGIAR’s ICARDA and Bioversity researchers** evaluated rangeland restoration strategies in southern Tunisia (Tataouine), demonstrating that rest periods and sustainable land management improve plant cover, biomass, and species persistence under climate stress (hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/2ZQCH3, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/QBZTMV, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/ZC9K8J, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/ZAJDIV, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/X9IYIU, hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/IKPUUB).

• **CGIAR’s ICARDA** assessed the vulnerability of native rangeland species (e.g., *Haloxylon schmittianum*) to climate change, proposing grazing pressure management as an adaptation strategy to reduce competition for water and nutrients (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** simulated climate change impacts on hard wheat productivity in Tunisia, testing adaptation strategies (e.g., increased irrigation/fertilization) and highlighting water availability as a critical constraint (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154919).

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** surveyed COVID-19 impacts on smallholder crop-livestock households in Zaghouan, documenting farmers’ perceptions of productivity losses and planned structural land-use changes to cope with shocks (hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/1NXHGT).
Tunisia.water:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** analyzed the role of water in climate adaptation for hard wheat systems, showing that increased irrigation/fertilization could offset productivity losses under mild warming (1°C) but remains dependent on water accessibility (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154919).

• **CGIAR’s ICARDA** identified reduced competition for water and nutrients as a resilience advantage for low-palatability rangeland species (e.g., *Haloxylon* spp.) under climate change (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-013-0701-z).

South Sudan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **East Africa (including South Sudan)** through evidence-based climate projections, capacity-building, and policy-aligned research. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers** quantified climate-migration risks by linking drought frequency to outmigration trends in East Africa (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278**).
- **The Center** analyzed climate extremes’ impacts on agricultural production and food security, highlighting risks for farmers (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106796**).
South Sudan.adaptation:
• **CGIAR researchers** demonstrated that longer droughts and climate extremes will exacerbate vulnerability in East African agricultural livelihoods, correlating dry days with increased emigration (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278**).
• **The Center** documented climate change’s direct/indirect impacts on agricultural value chains, food security, and sustainable growth in Eastern Africa (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106796**).
• **CGIAR’s AGNES initiative** strengthened African negotiators’ capacity to attribute climate extremes to GHG emissions, supporting evidence-based climate adaptation (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443**).
• **The Center** facilitated a **South-South workshop** (2021) to co-produce climate information for agriculture/food security, enhancing seasonal forecasting and institutional linkages (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116941**).
• **CGIAR’s UNHCR-aligned work** developed a **Regional Climate Action Plan (2023–2028)** for East/Horn of Africa, integrating climate adaptation for displaced populations (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370**).
• **ILRI/CGIAR** partnered on **livestock and pastoralism research** for climate adaptation in the IGAD region (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053**).
• **CGIAR’s maize systems research** modeled yield reductions under climate change (2050/2080) and proposed adaptation measures for sub-Saharan Africa (Source: **10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005**).
South Sudan.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s AGNES initiative** trained African negotiators in **attribution science** to link GHG emissions to climate extremes, informing mitigation strategies (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443**).

Qatar.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate adaptation and mitigation in agriculture, landscapes, and forest ecosystems** during COP18 in Doha, Qatar, through high-level policy engagement, thematic assessments, and expert-led discussions. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** led evaluations of national adaptation programs in West Africa, highlighting progress and challenges (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74865).
- **ICARDA and CGIAR researchers** convened global panels to address climate impacts on agriculture, livelihoods, and scalable solutions (Sources https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74852, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74853).
- **IUFRO and CGIAR experts** advanced **REDD+ and forest management** as dual strategies for **mitigation (carbon sequestration)** and **adaptation (resilience)** (Source https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74955).
Qatar.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** conducted a **special report** on West African national adaptation programs (Burkina Faso, Mali, Niger), assessing progress and resilience gaps one year before program closure (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74865).
• **Agriculture, Landscapes and Livelihoods Day (CGIAR/ICARDA)** hosted high-level panels to identify **scalable solutions** for climate-vulnerable agricultural systems, featuring global experts and policymakers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74852, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74853).
• **CGIAR emphasized agriculture as a critical adaptation lever** in COP18 reports, advocating for its integration into climate strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74870).
Qatar.mitigation: • **CGIAR (via IUFRO collaboration)** presented **REDD+ and forest management** as key mitigation tools at COP18’s All Forest Day, linking carbon sequestration to biodiversity and resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/74955).

Bolivia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agricultural adaptation** in **Bolivia** through **genetic conservation, pest risk modeling, and farmer-led resilience strategies**. Specifically:
- The **International Potato Center (CIP)** and **CCAFS** validated climate threats to potato agriculture (e.g., temperature shifts, extreme events) and proposed **drought- and heat-resilient potato varieties**, improved water management, and *ex situ/in situ* genetic conservation (e.g., at **Parque de la Papa**) to safeguard livelihoods (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65725**).
- **Bioversity International** and **CCAFS** demonstrated that **agricultural biodiversity** (e.g., native potato varieties) enhances smallholder adaptive capacity, based on surveys of 2,400+ farmers across Bolivia, India, and Nepal (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/52109**).
Bolivia.water: • **CCAFS** and **CIP** identified **improved water management practices** as critical for adapting potato cultivation to **climatic variability** (e.g., altered precipitation patterns), though no standalone water-focused mitigation strategies were documented (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65725**).

Argentina.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA), mitigation of greenhouse gas emissions, and adaptive breeding** in **Argentina** through **digital tools, genetic innovation, and agro-climatic forecasting**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** developed drought-tolerant bean varieties via regionalized breeding networks, supporting Argentina’s semiarid zones (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S7LJBI).
- **FLAR** empowered Argentina’s rice sector with **digital CSA decision tools** via the *Rice Observatory* platform, covering **2 million hectares** (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121433).
- **CCAFS** advanced **climate-site-specific agronomy** by equipping farmer organizations with seasonal forecasts and data-driven agronomic recommendations (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121686).
Argentina.adaptation:
• **CIAT** designed the **VEF(IBN)-EP-IBYAN scheme** to distribute drought-tolerant bean germplasm to Argentina’s Northwest region, addressing major production constraints through a **regional division of labor** (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/S7LJBI).
• **CCAFS** enabled **10 farmer organizations in Latin America (including Argentina)** with **climate-site-specific agronomy tools**, integrating seasonal agro-climatic forecasts to reduce climate vulnerability (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121686).
• **CCAFS** democratized **digital Climate Smart Agriculture (D-CSA)** tools, improving yields for **100,000+ farmers** via open data-driven platforms (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121856).
Argentina.mitigation:
• **CGIAR researchers** compiled **nitrous oxide (N₂O) emission data** from urine patches in Argentine degraded/improved pastures, analyzing **N₂O fluxes, soil nitrogen, and emission factors** (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BM0ILQ).
• **CLIFF-GRADS (CCAFS/GRA)** facilitated **Low Emissions Development research** in Argentina, linking alumni like **Florencia Garcia** to global agricultural GHG mitigation networks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109048).
Argentina.water:
• **FLAR (CGIAR)** integrated Argentina into the **Latin American Rice Observatory**, providing **digital tools for Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) decisions** across **2 million hectares** of irrigated rice (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121433).
• **CGIAR climate modeling** (via **CMIP5 projections**) identified **increased monsoon precipitation** in **northern Argentina** under high-emission scenarios, informing water-resource adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.1175/jcli-d-12-00412.1).

Gambia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive agriculture** in **The Gambia** through evidence-based policy frameworks, farmer-centered adaptation strategies, and ecosystem-based interventions. Specifically:
- **CIAT, IWMI, and IFPRI** evaluated **land and water management practices** to enhance farmer resilience to climate variability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).
- **CGIAR researchers** applied **Protection Motivation Theory** to analyze farmers’ adoption of water conservation and drought-tolerant measures in the Central River Region (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.3390/cli7010013).
Gambia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR (CIAT, IWMI, IFPRI)** conducted a **household survey** to assess **integrated land and water management** for climate adaptation, identifying scalable practices for West African smallholders, including Gambia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

• **CGIAR (AICCRA)** analyzed **20 Green Climate Fund agricultural adaptation projects**, including Gambia’s **FP011**, to categorize climate hazards, vulnerability, and response strategies using the **IPCC Risk Framework** (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ).

• **CGIAR researchers** used **Protection Motivation Theory** to study farmers’ adoption of **water conservation techniques** and **drought-tolerant crops**, highlighting barriers like cost and soil degradation in The Gambia’s Central River Region (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.3390/cli7010013).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS)** developed **Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) profiles** for The Gambia to guide **national agricultural investment plans**, aligning adaptation strategies with policy frameworks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121246).

• **CGIAR (ICRAF)** led a **biophysical soil and land health assessment** in the **Gambia River Basin**, supporting **Large-Scale Ecosystem-Based Adaptation** to build climate-resilient natural resource economies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/IGJZLF).

• **CGIAR researchers** explored **farm-level adaptation options**, including **water conservation techniques** and **chemical fertilizers**, correlating soil storage capacity with resilience in The Gambia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.3390/atmos10070423).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS)** synthesized **climate-smart agriculture strategies** for West Africa, reviewing adaptation policies for **crop, livestock, and fishery sectors** to improve farming system resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
Gambia.water:
• **CGIAR (CIAT, IWMI, IFPRI)** evaluated **land and water management practices** to enhance resilience, including **water conservation techniques** for smallholder farmers in Gambia (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/2PZOPV).

• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **soil water storage capacity** and **water conservation adoption** as critical adaptation measures in The Gambia’s agricultural systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.3390/cli7010013, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.3390/atmos10070423).

Togo.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and inclusive climate action** in **Togo** through capacity-building, participatory projects, and resilience-focused interventions. Specifically:
- **CIAT and Bioversity International** developed participatory, village-based CSA training manuals and field visits to integrate theoretical and practical learning in Togo (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/144087**).
- **CCAFS West Africa** assessed the climate-smartness of the **West Africa Agricultural Productivity Programme (WAAPP)** in Togo, strengthening stakeholder capacity to mainstream CSA (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455**).
- **CaSCIERA-WA** expanded CSA tools and climate information use in Togo’s agricultural sector, tailoring interventions to projected climate risks (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121338**).
Togo.adaptation:
• **CIAT and Bioversity International** formulated and implemented **participatory, village-based Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) projects** in Togo, including training manuals and field visits to demonstrate CSA practices (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/144087**).

• **CCAFS West Africa** conducted **participatory assessments** of WAAPP projects in Togo to evaluate climate-smartness and strengthen stakeholder capacity for mainstreaming CSA (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455**).

• **AICCRA** expanded the **Independent Global Stocktake (iGST) West Africa Hub** to Togo, fostering inclusive climate action and scaling CSA technologies through civil society engagement (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179547**).

• **CaSCIERA-WA** mainstreamed CSA into Togo’s agricultural sector by integrating **climate information (past, prevailing, and projected)** to enhance resilience in two communes (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121338**).

• **CGIAR researchers** reviewed **climate change impacts and adaptation strategies** for crop, livestock, and fishery sectors in West Africa, including Togo, to inform policy and institutional mechanisms for climate-smart agriculture (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3**).
Togo.mitigation: • **AICCRA** capacitated **West African civil society organizations (CSOs)**, including those in Togo, to mobilize climate finance (e.g., Green Climate Fund) by designing bankable concept notes for locally relevant **adaptation and mitigation actions** (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179563**).

Chad.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive capacity** in **Chad** through evidence-based datasets, climate-smart agriculture (CSA) assessments, and capacity-building for negotiators. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s West Africa regional program** integrated large-scale household food security surveys to analyze coping strategies and climate shocks (e.g., extreme weather, conflict) in Chad (2018–2023) (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SQA143).
- **CGIAR-CCAFS** assessed the climate-smartness of WAAPP projects in Chad, strengthening stakeholder capacity to mainstream CSA (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455).
- **AGNES (via CGIAR partnerships)** trained African negotiators—including Chad’s representatives—in attribution science to link climate extremes to greenhouse gas emissions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).
Chad.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s West Africa program** developed a standardized dataset on household food security in Chad, linking **climate hazards (e.g., extreme weather, rainy season performance)** to coping strategies and vulnerability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SQA143).
• **CGIAR-CCAFS** evaluated the **climate-smartness of WAAPP projects** in Chad, focusing on stakeholder capacity to implement CSA and integrate climate information for agricultural resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106455).
• **CGIAR’s AGNES initiative** (in collaboration) conducted capacity-building on **climate change attribution** to empower Chad’s negotiators in understanding climate extremes and adaptation needs (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).
• **CGIAR researchers** reviewed **adaptation strategies for maize systems** in sub-Saharan Africa (including Chad’s agroecological zones), modeling yield reductions under climate change and proposing climate-smart interventions (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
• **CGIAR synthesized West African adaptation policies** for crops, livestock, and fisheries, including Chad’s context, to guide climate-smart agriculture adoption (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).
Chad.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s AGNES program** (via partnerships) advanced **attribution science** to quantify Chad’s exposure to climate extremes, supporting evidence-based mitigation negotiations at COP (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).

Japan.narrative: CGIAR’s research in Japan focused on **climate-smart agriculture, socio-ecological resilience, and greenhouse gas mitigation**, with contributions spanning **adaptation strategies, mitigation frameworks, and ecosystem-based solutions**. Specifically:
Japan.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** supported APEC economies—including Japan—in developing a **Multi-Year Action Plan for Climate Change and Food Security (2018–2021)**, facilitating stakeholder consultations and policy alignment (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197).
• **CCAFS and collaborators** analyzed **climate change projections and adaptation responses** in Asia, integrating case studies on vulnerability assessments and sector-specific strategies (e.g., water, agriculture, disaster management) to inform development cooperation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34939).
• **Researchers** explored **socio-ecological landscapes (e.g., *satoyama*)** in Japan, emphasizing **trade-offs in ecosystem services** (biodiversity, cultural heritage) and innovative management to sustain human–natural systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.34725/DVN/M2OEZT).
Japan.mitigation:
• **CCAFS and partners** (e.g., IRRI, MAFF Japan) documented **highlights, challenges, and opportunities** in reducing greenhouse gas emissions from **paddy rice systems**, a key agricultural mitigation focus (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/100227).
• **CGIAR contributed to climate-smart agriculture training** in Tokyo, presenting **mitigation options for agriculture** under the Asia Development Bank’s framework (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97790).

New Zealand.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient food systems and low-emission agricultural innovation** in **New Zealand** through **science-policy dialogues, greenhouse gas research, and regional partnerships**. Specifically:
- The Center hosted **Adaptation Futures 2025** in Ōtautahi Christchurch to advance **locally led, equitable climate adaptation** in food systems (Source_1).
- Researchers from **NZAGRC and CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security program** contributed to **global mitigation strategies**, including dairy sector GHG reductions and low-emission livestock systems (Sources_3, 5, 6, 7).
New Zealand.adaptation:
• CGIAR hosted **Adaptation Futures 2025** in Ōtautahi Christchurch, advancing **locally led, equitable climate-resilient food systems** through stakeholder co-creation and transformative systems change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/177760).
• The Center supported **APEC economies** in developing a **multi-year climate change and food security plan (2018–2021)**, including stakeholder consultations and policy alignment (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197).
New Zealand.mitigation:
• CGIAR researchers (e.g., **NZAGRC’s Stewart Ledgard**) presented **New Zealand dairy sector GHG reduction strategies** at global mitigation forums, informing low-emission development options (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110571).
• The Center facilitated **East African partnerships** to reduce livestock GHG emissions, leveraging New Zealand’s expertise (e.g., **NZAGRC’s Dr. Ackim Mwape**) in policy and science (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121310).
• CGIAR’s **Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security program** advanced **agricultural climate action** via global collaborations, including **Climate and Clear Air Coalition (CCAC) workshops** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109424).
• The **NZAGRC** contributed to **CLIFF-GRADS Science Collaboration Series**, fostering international GHG research networks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110095).

Timor-Leste.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation** in **Timor-Leste** through participatory action research and community-based assessment frameworks. Specifically:
- The Center developed a **four-step community-based adaptation assessment** to support decision-making for resilient fishing and farming communities (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69488).
- Researchers designed **Landscape Function Analysis (LFA)** to evaluate ecological functions of home garden systems as part of adaptation planning (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69495).
- CGIAR facilitated **Impact and Adaptation Assessment Workshops** to identify climate-sensitive livelihood risks and desirable future adaptation actions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69490).
- The Center mapped **ecosystem services** to assess natural resource impacts on fishing and farming resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69494).
- Researchers conducted **climate analysis** to identify long-term climate trends and ongoing adaptations in farming/fishing practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69489).
- CGIAR supported **implementation planning workshops** to guide community-led adaptation action (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69493).
Timor-Leste.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed a **community-based adaptation assessment framework** (four-step participatory process) to build resilience in coastal fishing and farming communities (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/69488).

Bhutan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agricultural adaptation** in **Bhutan** through collaborative research, policy integration, and capacity-building. Specifically:
- **CIAT** assessed climate change impacts on crop suitability (maize, rice, potato, chili, tomato) under high-emission scenarios (RCP 8.5) for 2050, while training local staff to generate suitability maps and introduce climate-resilient crop management (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HGECWU).
- **Bioversity International** piloted a **seed resilience strategy** to enhance farmers’ access to crop diversity, combining climate modeling and participatory methods to strengthen adaptive capacity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).
- **CCAFS (Climate Change Agriculture and Food Security)** facilitated regional knowledge exchange on **climate-smart agriculture (CSA)**, scaling policy frameworks for Bhutan’s agricultural strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123200).
- **IFPRI** supported Bhutan’s **Renewable Natural Resource (RNR) Strategy** (2018–2040) by aligning agricultural development with climate-smart paradigms (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121307).
Bhutan.adaptation:
• **CIAT** evaluated climatic suitability shifts for key crops (maize, rice, potato, chili, tomato) under RCP 8.5 (2050) and trained Bhutanese stakeholders in climate-resilient crop management (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HGECWU).
• **Bioversity International** developed a **seed resilience strategy** to diversify crop varieties, enabling farmers to adapt to climate variability through participatory research and policy support (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/78539).
• **CCAFS** advanced **climate-smart agricultural systems** in Bhutan by mainstreaming climate variability into national policies and scaling CSA practices via South-South collaboration (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106626).
• **IFPRI** integrated **Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) technologies** into Bhutan’s **Agricultural Development Plan (2040)**, aligning with evolving environmental dynamics (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121307).
• **CCAFS** adapted a **government expenditure framework** for Bhutan to prioritize CSA scaling, informed by India’s policy reviews (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123200).
• **CGIAR researchers** (via regional review) emphasized **hybrid adaptive knowledge systems** (local + scientific) to enhance coping mechanisms in Bhutan’s highland agriculture (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7).

Eritrea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation and agricultural resilience** in **Eritrea and the East/Horn of Africa** through **policy frameworks, research-for-development partnerships, and climate-projection modeling**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s ILRI and regional centers** advanced **livestock and pastoralism adaptation strategies** for climate-vulnerable communities (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053**).
- **CGIAR researchers** quantified **climate-migration linkages**, demonstrating how droughts and dry-day increases correlate with youth outmigration in East Africa (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278**).
- **CGIAR contributed to the UNHCR Climate Action Plan (2023–2028)** for the East/Horn of Africa, aligning with IGAD’s **regional climate adaptation and mitigation frameworks** (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370**).
- **CGIAR’s bioeconomic modeling** (via maize systems research) assessed **climate impacts on food security** in sub-Saharan Africa, including Eritrea’s **maize mega-environments**, and proposed **adaptation measures for crop varieties** (Source: **10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005**).
Eritrea.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s ILRI and CGIAR centers** partnered with EU programs to **develop climate-adaptive livestock and pastoralism interventions** in the IGAD region, including Eritrea (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/128053**).

• **CGIAR researchers** analyzed **climate extremes (droughts, dry days) and their direct/indirect impacts on agricultural livelihoods**, linking increased climate variability to **higher migration risks** among East African youth (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/110278**).

• **CGIAR supported the UNHCR Climate Action Plan (2023–2028)**, integrating **climate adaptation measures for forcibly displaced populations** and aligning with IGAD’s **Regional Climate Change Strategy (2023–2030)** (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370**).

• **CGIAR’s maize systems research** evaluated **biophysical and socioeconomic climate impacts** on staple crops, identifying **region-specific adaptation needs** (e.g., drought-resistant varieties) for Eastern Africa’s **maize mega-environments** (Source: **10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005**).

• **CGIAR, via the UNHCR-led Regional Climate Action Plan (2023–2028)**, contributed to developing **targeted climate adaptation measures** for vulnerable populations in Djibouti, aligned with IGAD’s Regional Climate Change Strategy (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/137370).

• **CGIAR’s Advanced AGNES Courses** trained African negotiators—including Djibouti’s representatives—in **attribution science** to link greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes, enhancing evidence-based climate adaptation strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).

Gabon.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and food security** in **Gabon** through **capacity-building for negotiators** and **climate-adaptive maize systems**. Specifically:
- **AGNES (Advanced AGNES Courses)** strengthened African negotiators’ technical expertise in **attribution science** to link greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes, empowering Gabon’s role in climate negotiations (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443**).
- **CIAT (International Center for Tropical Agriculture)** modeled **climate change impacts on maize yields** in sub-Saharan Africa, including Gabon’s agroecological zones, to inform adaptation strategies for food security (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005**).
Gabon.adaptation:

Liberia.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **Liberia** focused on **climate adaptation** for cocoa and agricultural systems through vulnerability assessments, climate-proofing strategies, and data-driven prioritization of investments. Specifically:
- **CIAT** conducted climate impact studies on cocoa to inform **$75 million** in IFAD investments, recommending region-specific adaptation strategies (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76385**).
- **CGIAR’s AICCRA project** contributed Liberia’s **Green Climate Fund (GCF) adaptation project (FP160)** to the dataset on climate hazards, vulnerability, and response strategies under the **IPCC Risk Framework** (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ**).
Liberia.adaptation:
• **CIAT** prioritized **$4.5 million** in IFAD-funded adaptation measures for Liberia’s cocoa sector, using climate impact assessments to design region-specific interventions (e.g., climate-proofing) and inform the **Adaptation for Smallholder Agriculture Programme (ASAP)** (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76385**).

• **CGIAR’s AICCRA** included Liberia’s **GCF adaptation project (FP160)** in a cross-country dataset analyzing climate hazards, vulnerability, and adaptive responses under the **IPCC Risk Framework**, supporting evidence-based project design (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ**).

• **MaxEnt modeling (CIAT/CCAFS)** identified Liberia as a **low-vulnerability zone** for cocoa under climate change (compared to forest-savanna transitions in Nigeria/Côte d’Ivoire), recommending **shade-tree restoration** as a critical adaptation strategy to offset projected temperature stress (Sources: **https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/5w7lgw**, **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/5W7LGW**).

- The Center developed **climate exposure maps and climate-smart practices** for cocoa resilience (Source_1).
- Researchers contributed to **regional CSA policy frameworks** to ensure robustness against climate variability (Source_2).
- CGIAR advised on **greenhouse gas inventories** to mitigate agricultural sector emissions (Source_3).

Papua New Guinea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptation** in **Papua New Guinea (PNG)** through evidence-based policy support and gender-inclusive climate services. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** analyzed PNG’s climate vulnerability, highlighting limited adaptive capacity due to high agricultural dependence (69% employment, 36% GDP) and risks from extreme weather (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/145066**).
- **CCAFS** facilitated stakeholder consultations to develop a **Multi-Year Action Plan (2018–2021)** for climate change and food security in APEC economies, including PNG (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197**).
- **CGIAR researchers** reviewed gender disparities in rural climate services, identifying barriers (e.g., ICT access, socio-cultural norms) and recommending women’s group inclusion and tailored ICT solutions to improve climate information equity (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216**).
Papua New Guinea.adaptation:

• **CCAFS** supported APEC economies—including PNG—in designing a **Multi-Year Action Plan (2018–2021)** for climate-resilient food systems through stakeholder consultations and policy alignment (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197**).
• **CGIAR’s gender and climate team** analyzed inequalities in rural climate services, proposing **women’s group integration** and **ICT-based solutions** to address gender gaps in climate information access and risk management (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216**).

Tajikistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and agricultural adaptation** in **Tajikistan** through stakeholder coordination, genetic biodiversity conservation, and rangeland management. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** preserved genetic diversity of *Juglans regia* (walnut) by analyzing wild and cultivated populations across Central Asia (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH).
- **ICARDA** mapped institutional stakeholders to strengthen **NDC implementation** and vertical coordination for climate action (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138814).
Tajikistan.adaptation:
• **CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** mapped **institutional stakeholders** to enhance coordination for **NDC implementation**, addressing climate vulnerability in Tajikistan’s policy and development frameworks (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/138814).

• **ICARDA** assessed **climate change impacts on farm income security** in Tajikistan’s arid zones, highlighting risks from **irrigation water scarcity** and modeling adaptation options for small-scale farmers (Source: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033).

• **CGIAR’s Soil Management Program** provided **long-term weather data (1936–2018)** for Khujand, Tajikistan, to simulate **climate change impacts on agricultural productivity** and evaluate adaptation technologies (Source: hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/FEQGGW).

• **ICARDA** studied **grazing responses in mountain rangelands**, documenting biomass dynamics of drought-tolerant species (*Haloxylon persicum*, *Ferula foetida*) under seasonal livestock pressure (Source: hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/AFAJPH).
Tajikistan.water: • **CGIAR’s CCAFS** analyzed **irrigation water availability risks** due to climate change, modeling negative impacts on Tajikistan’s arid-zone agriculture and upstream water demand conflicts (Source: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033).

Malaysia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive strategies in Southeast Asia**, including Malaysia, through **stakeholder-driven research, policy support, and community-based interventions**. Specifically:

- **CCAFS SEA** developed **Climate-Smart Village (CSV) frameworks** to enhance rural resilience, with Malaysia participating in regional workshops to implement the model (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123058**).

• **CCAFS SEA** organized **regional conferences (e.g., Hanoi, 2018)** to strengthen **research-for-development (R4D) collaborations** among ASEAN nations, including Malaysia, to combat climate threats through policy and technical exchanges (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101149**).

• **CCAFS SEA** outlined **2019–2021 R4D thrusts** focused on **climate-smart agriculture (CSA)** and **food security integration**, with Malaysia included in flagship CSV projects to enhance adaptive capacities (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/99018**).

Namibia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate resilience and sustainable land management** in **Namibia** through evidence-based adaptation strategies, land degradation neutrality (LDN) baselines, and climate risk assessments. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Research Program** developed **LDN baselines** for soil organic carbon, land productivity, and bush encroachment in Otjozondjupa (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FA3ZJS).
- **The Center** mapped **climate hazards, vulnerability, and adaptive responses** for Namibia’s Green Climate Fund (GCF) projects under the **IPCC Risk Framework** (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ).
Namibia.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Research Program** established **LDN baselines** (land cover change, soil organic carbon, bush encroachment) in **Otjozondjupa** to support sustainable land productivity and SDG 15.3 (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FA3ZJS).

• **CGIAR’s Green Climate Fund (GCF) team** analyzed **climate hazards, exposure, and adaptive strategies** for Namibia’s agricultural projects (FP023, FP024, SAP001) using the **IPCC Risk Framework**, informing data-driven adaptation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PY3YOZ).

• **CGIAR researchers** documented **climate-induced displacement and social tensions** in Namibia, linking livelihood risks to **climate peace and mobility** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173622).

• **CGIAR’s maize systems research** (via **CIMMYT/IITA collaboration**) modeled **climate change impacts on maize yields** in Namibia’s **maize mega-environments (MMEs)**, identifying adaptation measures for 2050/2080 under A1B scenarios (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
Namibia.mitigation:

• **CGIAR’s Land Degradation Neutrality (LDN) team** developed **land use/land cover (LUC) change baselines (2001–2017)** for Omusati, linking mitigation to **carbon sequestration and sustainable land use** (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XZIRK0).

- CGIAR reviewed **gender-responsive rural climate services** to address disparities in access and use (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1080/17565529.2019.1613216**).
- CGIAR evaluated **climate-smart policy frameworks** to align institutional arrangements for mitigation and adaptation (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-810521-4.00012-8**).

France.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA)** and **adaptation-mitigation synergies** in **France** through policy guidance, global scientific collaboration, and regional resilience frameworks. Specifically:
- The Center developed the **Climate-Smart Agriculture Investment Plan (CSAIP) Development Guide** to align global adaptation and mitigation efforts under the **Paris Agreement (COP21, 2015)** (Sources: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106259*).
- Researchers at **CCAFS (Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security)** advanced **climate-resilient livestock productivity** (goats/sheep) and **indigenous knowledge integration** in East Africa, while framing CSA as a priority for **COP21 negotiations** (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68858*).
- The **3rd Global Science Conference on CSA (Montpellier, 2015)** mobilized **600+ researchers** to formalize the **Montpellier Statement**, linking **food security, adaptation, and mitigation** under sustainable landscapes (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67193*).
France.adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **CCAFS** highlighted **climate-resilient livestock systems** (goats/sheep productivity) under changing climatic conditions, leveraging **climate-smart villages (CSVs)** in East Africa as case studies (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68858*).
• The Center contributed to **COP21 (Paris, 2015)** by framing **adaptation strategies** for African agriculture, emphasizing **higher temperatures, uncertain precipitation, and extreme events** in national investment plans (Sources: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106259*).
• At the **Montpellier CSA Conference (2015)**, CGIAR researchers confirmed **adaptation solutions** exist but require **policy alignment** to scale (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67193*).
France.mitigation:
• CGIAR developed the **CSAIP Development Guide** to **reduce greenhouse gas emissions** while integrating **adaptation** into **Climate-Smart Agriculture Investment Plans**, directly responding to the **Paris Agreement’s mitigation goals** (Sources: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106262*, *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106259*).
• The **Montpellier Statement (2015)** positioned agriculture as a **central mitigation sector**, advocating for **GHG emission reductions** through **sustainable landscapes and food systems** (Source: *https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67193*).

• **CMIP5 climate modeling (CGIAR-affiliated research)** projected **increased monsoon precipitation (30% amplitude rise by 2045–50)** and **extended wet-season durations** over southern Brazil, Uruguay, and northern Argentina, informing water-resource planning (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1175/jcli-d-12-00412.1**).

Angola.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture and agroclimatic risk assessment** in **Angola** through data-driven tools and capacity-building initiatives. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Agroclimatic Research Center** developed high-resolution agroclimatic indices (e.g., SPEI, heatwave metrics) for maize, beans, soybeans, and coffee to quantify water balance, dry/wet spells, and crop-specific vulnerabilities (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BA6QVX).
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** (via AGNES) strengthened African negotiators’ technical capacity in attribution science to link greenhouse gas emissions to climate extremes, supporting evidence-based climate policy in Angola (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).
- **CGIAR’s Maize Program** modeled biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize yields (2050/2080) under varying nitrogen inputs, identifying adaptation strategies for Angola’s maize mega-environments (Source: https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
Angola.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Program** (via AGNES) trained African negotiators—including Angolan representatives—in attribution science to strengthen climate resilience negotiations, focusing on linking emissions to extreme impacts (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/178443).
• **CGIAR’s Maize Program** assessed climate change impacts on maize yields in Angola’s mega-environments (2050/2080) under the A1B scenario, identifying region-specific adaptation measures (e.g., improved varieties, input optimization) to mitigate yield reductions (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005).
Angola.mitigation: • **No evidence found in this sample** for direct mitigation-focused research (e.g., GHG reduction strategies, carbon sequestration) in Angola.
Angola.water: • **CGIAR’s Agroclimatic Research Center** developed a 0.05°-resolution dataset of **13 agroclimatic indices** (e.g., SPEI, potential evapotranspiration, dry/wet spells) for Angola’s maize, beans, soybeans, and coffee regions, enabling water-balance analysis and drought/heatwave risk assessment (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BA6QVX).

Haiti.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate risk profiling and participatory climate services** in **Haiti** through evidence-based adaptation strategies. Specifically:
- The Center developed **Climate Risk Profiles** to assess current and future climate hazards (e.g., precipitation, temperature) for Haiti (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0).
- Researchers supported the **scaling of Participatory Integrated Climate Services for Agriculture (PICSA)**—a methodology tested and expanded to Haiti as part of a 17-country initiative (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121373).
Haiti.adaptation:
• CGIAR developed **Climate Risk Profiles** for Haiti, mapping current and future climate hazards (precipitation, temperature) to inform adaptation planning (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0).
• The Center contributed to **scaling PICSA (Participatory Integrated Climate Services for Agriculture)**, a farmer-centric methodology tested in Haiti alongside 16 other countries, reaching intermediaries and smallholders (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121373).

Guinea-Bissau.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) and climate risk profiling** in **Guinea-Bissau** through **resilience-building and adaptive maize systems**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture research** advanced integrated productivity, adaptation, and mitigation strategies to enhance food security under climate change (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106070**).
- **CGIAR’s World Food Program collaboration** mapped climate hazards (precipitation, temperature) to inform risk profiles for Guinea-Bissau (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PKZ0G0**).
Guinea-Bissau.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture team** designed frameworks to **sustainably increase productivity, enhance resilience, and reduce climate risks** through farmer-adopted practices, addressing trade-offs between adaptation and mitigation (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106070**).

• **CGIAR’s bioeconomic modeling (maize systems)** quantified **climate change impacts on maize yields (2050/2080)** under varying nitrogen levels, identifying **adaptation measures for Western Africa’s maize mega-environments** (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005**).

• **CGIAR’s West Africa review** synthesized **climate change impacts and adaptation strategies** for crops, livestock, and fisheries, informing **policy and institutional mechanisms** to improve farming resilience (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3**).
Guinea-Bissau.mitigation: • **CGIAR’s Climate-Smart Agriculture research** explored **GHG reduction/removal** as a core pillar of CSA, alongside productivity and adaptation, to align with global food systems goals (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106070**).

Kazakhstan.narrative:
CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS) program advanced climate-smart agricultural strategies in Kazakhstan through mitigation-focused technical and financial innovations. Specifically:
- **CCAFS** provided technical guidance to the World Bank and Asian Development Bank on **low-emissions livestock production** and **soil carbon monitoring**, directly informing the **USD 500 million Kazakhstan Sustainable Livestock Development Program** (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121203, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/124646).
Kazakhstan.adaptation: • **CGIAR** used the **bio-economic farm model (BEFM)** to assess **sub-national climate change impacts** on farm income security across Central Asia, including Kazakhstan, highlighting adaptation needs for small-scale farms in arid zones and market-based solutions to mitigate risks (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2014.02.033).
Kazakhstan.mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** supported **low-emissions sustainable livestock production** and **soil carbon crediting frameworks**, enabling investments like the **USD 500 million Kazakhstan Sustainable Livestock Development Program** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121203).
• **CCAFS** developed **soil carbon monitoring methods** to validate mitigation efforts in Kazakhstan’s livestock sector (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/124646).

Korea.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation and food security strategy** in **Korea** through **modeling, policy support, and multi-stakeholder engagement**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR (via IMPACT modeling)** assessed future food security trends under climate change adaptation strategies, projecting kilocalorie availability and import dependence (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/146482**).
- **CGIAR’s CCAFS (CIAT, CIP, RPL SEA)** facilitated the **APEC Multi-Year Action Plan (2018–2021)** on climate change and food security, organizing stakeholder consultations and drafting policy priorities (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197**).
Korea.adaptation:
• **CGIAR (IMPACT model)** evaluated Korea’s agricultural adaptation strategies, projecting food security resilience despite climate shocks and dietary shifts (e.g., reduced arable land, increased imports) (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/146482**).
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** supported APEC economies in designing a **multi-year climate-food security plan (2018–2021)**, including stakeholder-driven priorities and policy frameworks (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121197**).

Uzbekistan.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate adaptation and agroecosystem resilience** in **Uzbekistan** through genetic, agronomic, and rangeland research. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** conducted genetic analyses of *Juglans regia* (walnut) populations to assess biodiversity and adaptation in wild and cultivated trees (Source A).
- **ICARDA** modeled climate impacts on agricultural productivity and tested adaptation technologies using long-term weather data from Karshi, Uzbekistan (Source B).
- **ICARDA** evaluated vegetation responses to livestock grazing in Kyzylkum desert rangelands to inform sustainable rangeland management (Source C).
- **ICARDA** piloted **Farming with Alternative Pollinators (FAP)** to enhance climate resilience and yields in diverse agroecosystems (Source D).
Uzbekistan.adaptation:
• **Bioversity International** analyzed **genetic diversity and adaptation** of *Juglans regia* (walnut) across wild and cultivated populations in Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, and Kyrgyzstan to support climate-resilient tree breeding (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VUP7OH).
• **ICARDA** generated **long-term climate data (1936–2018)** for Karshi, Uzbekistan, to simulate agricultural productivity losses and test adaptation technologies (hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/FEQGGW).
• **ICARDA** assessed **vegetation degradation gradients** in Kyzylkum rangelands due to livestock grazing, using a transect-based approach to quantify biomass and species composition changes (hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/L7XSN8).
• **ICARDA** tested **Farming with Alternative Pollinators (FAP)** as a climate-resilience strategy, comparing yields in agroecosystems with monoculture controls (hdl:20.500.11766.1/FK2/FH2FJU).

Mauritania.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate change adaptation and resilience** in **Mauritania** through **scenario-based planning and youth engagement**. Specifically:
- The Center facilitated **scenario-building workshops** to assess climate impacts on fisheries and aquaculture, integrating youth perspectives and creative thinking into adaptation strategies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34904).
- Researchers developed **country-specific fisheries sector scenarios** for Mauritania and Senegal to inform climate adaptation policies, addressing localized productivity changes and vulnerability (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34905).
Mauritania.adaptation:
• CGIAR organized **participatory foresight workshops** in Mauritania, Senegal, and Ghana to construct **2050 scenarios** for fisheries/aquaculture, emphasizing youth inclusion and creative adaptation planning (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34904).
• The Center designed **climate-resilient fisheries sector scenarios** for Mauritania, analyzing non-climatic drivers of change to strengthen adaptation policies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/34905).
• Researchers reviewed **climate-smart agriculture strategies** for West Africa, including Mauritania, to enhance farming system resilience through policy and institutional mechanisms (https://doi.org/10.1186/s40066-016-0075-3).

Iraq.narrative: CGIAR **has no documented research contributions in Iraq** within this sample of high-impact records, as all provided sources focus exclusively on U.S.-centric political polling (e.g., elections, public opinion on the Iraq War) with **no climate-related terms** (water, adaptation, mitigation, crops, livestock, or production systems).

Afghanistan.narrative:
