- **ICARDA** facilitated participatory selection and promotion of **drought- and heat-tolerant wheat landraces** to enhance food security and climate resilience (hdl:11529/10548355).
- **CGIAR researchers** evaluated **hybrid adaptive knowledge systems** (combining local and scientific expertise) to strengthen community-based climate adaptation in the Asian Highlands (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-014-1090-7).

• **ICARDA** developed and disseminated **drought- and heat-tolerant wheat landraces** through participatory breeding, disease-resistant crosses, and farmer training to enhance resilience in Afghanistan and neighboring regions (hdl:11529/10548355).

- **CCAFS (FP2, RPL SEA, CIAT)** facilitated a multi-year climate-food security plan for APEC economies (Source 2).

Lesotho.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-smart agriculture and adaptation strategies** in **Lesotho** through **bioeconomic modeling and participatory climate services**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** assessed biophysical and socioeconomic impacts of climate change on maize production, identifying yield reductions and adaptation measures under varying input levels (Source: [https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005](https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-01-2014-0005)).
- **CCAFS** scaled out the **Participatory Integrated Climate Services for Agriculture (PICSA)** methodology, reaching farmers in Lesotho and 16 other countries to enhance climate resilience (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121373](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121373)).
Lesotho.adaptation:

• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** scaled the **PICSA approach** to Lesotho, training 140,850+ farmers across Africa/Asia/Latin America to improve climate resilience through participatory climate services (Source: [https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121373](https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121373)).

Antarctica.narrative:
CGIAR, in collaboration with the Columbia University Center for International Earth Science Information Network (CIESIN), contributed to global population modeling through **spatial data infrastructure**—**not** in Antarctica. Specifically:

- **CIAT** generated **GPWv3 Population Count Grids** (2005–2015) via proportional allocation algorithms, aligned with UN estimates (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/YS1DJO).

- **CRP WHEAT** conducted a **Genome-Wide Association Study (GWAS)** to identify drought- and heat-tolerant wheat landraces, integrating farmer knowledge and modern phenotyping in Iran (Maragheh) and Turkey (2015–2019) (Source: **hdl:11529/10548355**).
Iran.adaptation: • **CRP WHEAT** developed **drought- and heat-tolerant wheat landraces** through participatory selection, combining resilience traits (e.g., disease resistance to yellow rust, leaf rust, and stem rust) and adaptive agronomic practices in Iran (Maragheh, 2019) (**hdl:11529/10548355**).
Iran.mitigation: • **CRP WHEAT** promoted **sustainable wheat cultivation** by leveraging landrace biodiversity to mitigate climate change impacts, including training farmers and policymakers on conservation and adaptive practices (**hdl:11529/10548355**).

Mongolia.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient food systems and nutrition security in Mongolia** through evidence-based interventions. Specifically:

• **CGIAR implemented participatory research** across four agroecosystems, identifying **practical adaptation interventions** (e.g., risk management, value chain resilience) for livestock and vegetable producers (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodpol.2023.102468).

CGIAR’s research in **Puerto Rico** (Colombia) focused on **sustainable livestock adaptation** through geospatial data-driven technologies. Specifically:
- **CIAT** (Center for Tropical Agriculture) developed a **geospatial database of livestock producers** to map sustainable practices, including farm locations and producer profiles in Puerto Rico, supporting regional sustainability and conservation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/CHECU8).

general / cross-cutting.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **cross-cutting, climate-resilient agrifood systems** through evidence-based interventions and integrated assessments. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Evidence for Resilient Agriculture (ERA) initiative** synthesized 2,916 studies across Africa to evaluate climate-resilient agronomic practices, soil fertility management, and intercropping strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/C3YBNN).
- **The Transforming Agrifood Systems in South Asia (TAFSSA) initiative** conducted district-level assessments in Bangladesh, India, and Nepal to link climate risk responses, water access, and gender-inclusive natural resource management (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH).
- **Bioversity International and CIAT** developed financial feasibility maps for solar irrigation systems in Africa, comparing cost-efficiency under climate change scenarios (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/J0Q9TO).

• **CGIAR’s Evidence for Resilient Agriculture (ERA)** compiled 112,859 observations from 2,916 studies to assess climate-resilient agronomy, soil fertility, and intercropping in Africa (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/C3YBNN).
• **The SELEVER study (Burkina Faso)** evaluated an integrated poultry value chain intervention, linking adaptation (health/hygiene) with water management and women’s empowerment (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M4BTGB, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/E7QTZZ).
• **TAFSSA (South Asia)** assessed climate risk responses, water access, and resilience through gender-disaggregated household surveys in Bangladesh, India, and Nepal (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/V5FMHZ, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/Z0RF6L, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EN3RAD, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THVTYH).
general / cross-cutting.mitigation: • **CGIAR (IFPRI)** reviewed climate-smart agriculture policies and modeling to evaluate mitigation and adaptation strategies in agrifood systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/174179).

rice.narrative:
CGIAR’s research on rice prioritized **climate-resilient agriculture** through **modeling, pest-smart interventions, and gender-inclusive adaptation strategies**, particularly in **Southeast Asia and Africa**. Specifically:
- The Center developed **climate impact projections** for rice yields under divergent greenhouse gas scenarios (ISIMIP3b/CMIP6) using DSSAT models (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MXJDUC).
- Researchers at **IRRI** designed **pest-smart manuals and early warning systems** to address climate-induced pest/disease outbreaks in rice farming (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97537).
- The **Policy Information and Response Platform on Climate Change and Rice (PIRCCA)** analyzed **gender differences in adaptation strategies** across Vietnam’s Mekong Delta (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76928, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/70965).
rice.adaptation:
• CGIAR modeled **rice yield declines (0.24–0.9%)** under RCP 8.5 scenarios, projecting **food demand shifts** in the Philippines (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116413).
• The Center assessed **climate vulnerability in African tropical cereals**, including rice, highlighting **productivity declines** even at +2°C warming (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/66560).
• Researchers recalibrated **adaptation measures** for rice, emphasizing **water regulation, disease resistance, and agroforestry** as critical for recalibrating food production (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/24696).
• IRRI’s **PIRCCA project** supported **policy frameworks** for gender-inclusive climate adaptation in ASEAN rice systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/70965).
• A study on **Southeast Asian rice root architecture** identified **genetic traits (e.g., crown root number)** to improve drought resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ISBMO4).
rice.mitigation:
• CGIAR developed the **SECTOR GHG calculator**—a Tier 2 IPCC tool—to quantify **rice-specific emissions** and evaluate mitigation projects (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LKCBLZ).
• The Center compiled a **directory of climate finance sources** for **low-emissions paddy rice**, livestock, and agroforestry under Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions (NAMAs) (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/68682).
• Researchers analyzed **CO₂ fertilization effects** on rice yields via DSSAT, comparing scenarios with/without carbon uptake (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/MXJDUC).
rice.water:
• CGIAR’s studies emphasized **water management innovations** (e.g., regulating sporadic flooding) as key adaptation strategies for rice (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/24696).
• Root trait research highlighted **crown root number** as a drought-avoidance mechanism, with implications for **water-use efficiency** (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ISBMO4).

livestock (general/beef).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient and low-emission livestock systems** through **standardized data frameworks, policy coherence analyses, and forage innovations**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** expanded the **Animal Ontology for Livestock Trials (AOM v2)** to standardize feed trial metadata for diverse agro-ecological systems, enhancing resilience and farm management strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/75E7HV).
- **CGIAR Livestock and Climate Initiative** developed **improved forages** to boost drought tolerance and productivity while reducing greenhouse gas emissions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173226).
livestock (general/beef).adaptation:
• **CGIAR** developed the **Animal Ontology for Livestock Trials (AOM v2)**, a standardized framework to integrate livestock feed trial data across diverse agro-ecological systems, supporting resilience and adaptive farm management (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/75E7HV).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **livestock adaptation strategies** in nationally determined contributions (NDCs), highlighting priorities like **breed management, silvopastoralism, and feed management** to address climate risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115885).

• **CGIAR** assessed **climate change impacts on livestock disease vulnerability**, identifying that **58% of key livestock diseases are climate-sensitive**, requiring adaptive measures for smallholder systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/66595).

• **CGIAR** examined **policy coherence for livestock adaptation** in Kenya, Ethiopia, and Uganda, revealing gaps in integrating climate risks into livestock strategies despite progress in climate and development policies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101262).

• **CGIAR** summarized **adaptation options for African livestock keepers**, emphasizing the need for an **enabling technical and policy environment** to overcome constraints in adopting climate-resilient practices (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/66474).
livestock (general/beef).mitigation:
• **CGIAR** promoted **improved forages** as a dual solution for **climate change mitigation and adaptation**, reducing greenhouse gas emissions while enhancing livestock productivity in low- and middle-income countries (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/173226).

• **CGIAR** evaluated **livestock mitigation measures in NDCs**, showing that **34% of countries** included manure and feed management strategies, with **Tier 2 GHG inventory methods** as a tool to track emissions reductions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/115885, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76520).

• **CGIAR** analyzed **policy coherence for livestock mitigation** in East Africa, identifying **Ethiopia’s policies** as the most coherent but noting persistent gaps in adaptation-mitigation synergies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/101262).

• **CGIAR** developed the **CLEANED tool**, a rapid assessment framework to model **land use, water impacts, and GHG emissions** in livestock value chains (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4EB5XT).

• **CGIAR’s Evidence for Resilient Livestock (ERL) initiative** curated a **nutritional meta-dataset** to support emissions calculators and diet-based mitigation strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/JIU3OK).

coffee and cocoa.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers prioritized climate resilience and adaptive strategies for coffee and cocoa production systems through targeted interventions in Africa, Latin America, and Mesoamerica. Specifically:
- **CIAT/CIP** developed climate-smart frameworks to assess vulnerability and adaptation pathways for coffee and cocoa (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/TSUPE1, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/93348, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/125818).
- **Bioversity/CIAT** mapped agroclimatic suitability gradients to guide incremental and transformative adaptation strategies (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9QUGUR, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LXBZMU).
coffee and cocoa.adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **Climate Smart Cocoa (CSC) initiative** in Ghana and Côte d’Ivoire designed production systems to enhance resilience against droughts, storms, and flooding while improving farmer livelihoods through incremental and transformative adaptation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103770, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103790).

• **CIAT** quantified climate change impacts on coffee suitability in Central America and Mesoamerica, identifying a third of current production zones at risk of becoming unsuitable without adaptation, with higher-altitude regions requiring long-term transformative shifts (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/93348, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/TSUPE1).

• **CIAT/CIP** created a two-dimensional adaptation framework for coffee in Nicaragua, distinguishing short-term incremental strategies (e.g., low-altitude adjustments) from long-term transformative adaptations (e.g., higher-altitude shifts) (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/TSUPE1).

• **CIAT** developed agroclimatic zone (ZAC) models for cocoa in Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru to project future suitability and resilience, highlighting spatial gradients of impact (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/125818, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/LXBZMU).

• **Bioversity** assessed cocoa vulnerability in West Africa, identifying shade trees as critical adaptation strategies to mitigate dry-season temperature stress, reversing current trends of shade reduction (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/5w7lgw).

• **CIAT** mapped climate suitability for coffee in Central America using Random Forest models to guide private-sector engagement in Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) practices (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9QUGUR).

• **CGIAR’s Mesoamerican research** integrated climate models with livelihood assessments to identify vulnerability levels among coffee-growing families in Nicaragua, Guatemala, El Salvador, and Mexico, proposing targeted adaptation strategies (https://doi.org/10.7910/dvn/29418).

• **CIAT** quantified the economic costs of climate inaction on West African cocoa production, advocating for investments in adaptation to offset projected losses (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97166).
coffee and cocoa.mitigation: • CGIAR’s **Climate Smart Cocoa (CSC) initiative** in Ghana and Côte d’Ivoire integrated mitigation goals by aligning production systems with reduced greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions alongside resilience and productivity improvements (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103770, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103790).

roots and tubers.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers prioritized **climate resilience and adaptive strategies** for roots and tubers through targeted assessments, genetic improvement, and climate-smart interventions. Specifically:
- **CIAT** mapped global climate suitability for cassava cultivation, identifying edaphoclimatic zones based on temperature, dry season length, and seasonality (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WFAMUM).
- **CIP** conducted climate risk assessments for potato systems in Tanzania, Kenya, and Uganda, linking low yields to seed quality, agricultural practices, and market inefficiencies (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107442, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103233, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107721).
roots and tubers.adaptation:
• **CIAT** evaluated cassava landrace varieties across edaphoclimatic zones (Zones 2, 4, 5, 7) to identify genetic resilience to pests, diseases, and soil/climate stresses, informing recurrent breeding programs for yield, dry matter content, and pest-specific resistance (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/KM8CQG, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WXIGQY, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/FBX5DH, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/1K4GCI, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/L1TFKE).

• **CIP** assessed climate risks in potato systems (Tanzania, Kenya, Uganda), highlighting opportunities for climate-smart agriculture (CSA) to reduce losses, improve productivity, and enhance farmer incomes despite persistent challenges like low seed quality and marketing gaps (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107442, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103233, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107721).

• **CIAT/CIP** developed climate-yield models (e.g., PepsiCo’s Climate Resilience Platform) to translate climate risk data into actionable adaptation practices, strengthening supply-chain resilience for potato programs across 12 countries (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/152422).
roots and tubers.mitigation: • **CIAT/CIP** framed climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices as dual-purpose solutions to reduce agricultural losses *and* contribute to mitigation by improving productivity and farmer incomes (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103233, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107721).

legumes.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers, including **CIAT** and collaborators, advanced **climate-resilient legume systems** across Africa and Latin America through **adaptation-focused innovations** and **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) frameworks**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** led **climate risk assessments** for bean production in Kenya, identifying regional vulnerabilities and yield declines (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107723**).
- The Center designed **genomic prediction models** for drought- and phosphorus-stress tolerance in common beans, enabling trait-based breeding (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/XCD67U**).
legumes.adaptation:
• **CIAT** conducted **climate risk assessments** for legumes (soybeans, common beans, green grams) in **Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania**, quantifying production losses and proposing **climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices** to enhance resilience and productivity (Sources: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107723**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103239**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103235**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103232**).

• **CIAT & CGIAR-CCAFS** developed **AgroClimas**, a **climate vulnerability and risk management project** for artisanal bean seed production, integrating agroclimatic services for decision-making (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/80050**).

• Researchers **mapped abiotic/biotic constraints** across **176 sub-Saharan bean production areas**, standardizing data to inform targeted interventions (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/8W0QK7**).

• **CIAT** facilitated **farmer-led participatory trials** (e.g., *tricot* method) in **Nicaragua** to evaluate bean variety performance under abiotic stress, linking farmer observations to environmental data for adaptive breeding (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W**).

• The Center **prioritized dryland legumes** (chickpea, cowpea, pigeon pea) in global farming systems analysis, identifying **drought/heat hotspots** for research (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/EDMXSK**).
legumes.mitigation: • **No direct evidence of legume-specific mitigation studies** (e.g., carbon sequestration, GHG reductions) in this sample. **Indirect contributions** include CSA practices linked to **nitrogen fixation** (e.g., common beans in Tanzania) as part of broader mitigation strategies (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/103235**).
legumes.water: • **CIAT** tested **climate-smart farming practices** in **maize-legume systems**, measuring **soil water infiltration, moisture, and temperature** under variable weather to optimize productivity (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M0XCES**).

maize.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers prioritized **climate-resilient maize systems** across Latin America, Africa, and Asia through **adaptive modeling, digital agriculture, and climate-smart practices**. Specifically:
- **CIAT** modeled **climate change impacts on maize yields** in Guatemala and Honduras, projecting **12–14% yield losses** by 2050 and recommending policy interventions to enhance farmer productivity (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/146593, https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133215).
- **CIMMYT** deployed **digital sensor networks** in Guatemala to monitor soil and weather conditions, enabling data-driven improvements in maize productivity and sustainability (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DGLBH7).
- **ICRISAT** tested **climate-smart agricultural (CSA) practices** in Tanzania, linking soil moisture, nutrient management, and seasonal weather data to optimize maize-legume systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M0XCES).
maize.adaptation:
• **CIAT** projected **maize yield declines of 9–14% in Guatemala and Honduras** due to climate change, with farmers offsetting losses via increased productivity investments, and proposed **policy-driven adaptation strategies** for policymakers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/146593, https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.133215).
• **CIMMYT** developed **high-resolution climate projections for Ethiopia**, simulating **weather-event impacts on maize yields** to inform adaptive strategies for food security (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/THC08J).
• **CIAT** analyzed **historical climate trends in Nicaragua**, revealing maize’s **water-limited growth** (vs. beans) and documented **government-led adaptation responses**, including crop migration and export controls (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/29206).
• **ICRISAT** evaluated **climate-smart farming practices** in Tanzania, measuring **soil water infiltration, nutrient responses, and gross margins** to improve maize resilience under variable weather (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M0XCES).
• **CIAT** modeled **future climate impacts in Malawi (2040–2070)**, integrating **biophysical and economic frameworks** to assess maize yield risks for crop-livestock farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/149879).
• **CIAT** proposed **adaptation measures for Colombia’s maize supply chains**, analyzing climate risks and vulnerability reduction strategies (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/G9ADYV).
• **CIAT** highlighted **Zambia’s projected maize yield declines** due to water scarcity and extreme weather, emphasizing **risk management** in drought-prone regions (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136956).
maize.water:
• **CIAT** quantified maize’s **higher water limitations than beans** in Nicaragua due to its longer growing season, linking **deforestation-driven rainfall declines** to yield stagnation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/29206).
• **CIAT** projected **national water scarcity in Zambia**, warning of **reduced flows in the Zambezi, Kafue, and Luangwa basins** and their adverse effects on maize production (https://doi.org/10.2499/p15738coll2.136956).
• **CIMMYT** investigated **soil water infiltration and moisture dynamics** in Tanzania to optimize **climate-smart irrigation** for maize-legume systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/M0XCES).
• **IWMI/CIMMYT** analyzed **maize nutrient responses to water availability** in sub-Saharan Africa, revealing **variable profitability of fertilizer use** under water-limited conditions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/UNLRGC).

agroforestry.narrative: CGIAR prioritized **agroforestry** as a climate-resilient and mitigation-focused land-use strategy across Africa, Asia, and Latin America through policy development, institutional innovation, and scientific evidence generation. Specifically:
agroforestry.adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Change Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** developed community-based adaptation frameworks to enhance climate resilience in agriculture, emphasizing participatory approaches in Southeast Asia and West Africa (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/79434).
• **CGIAR (ICRAF)** contributed to **India’s National Agroforestry Policy (2014)**, a landmark policy mainstreaming agroforestry for climate risk reduction, productivity, and livelihood diversification (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/35592).
• **CGIAR (CCAFS Mali)** facilitated dialogue between researchers and policymakers to integrate climate-smart agriculture into national strategies, improving farm resilience in Sahelian agroforestry-pastoral systems (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/56669).
• **CGIAR (World Agroforestry Center)** mapped climate threats to 16 food tree species in Burkina Faso, identifying vulnerability hotspots for targeted adaptation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/3BTC8J).
• **CGIAR (Sustainable Amazonian Landscapes Project)** provided scientific evidence to support climate adaptation and sustainable land use in Colombia and Peru, linking agroecosystems to socio-economic benefits (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/BTRJKD).
agroforestry.mitigation:
• **CGIAR (ICRAF)** advanced **India’s National Agroforestry Policy** to leverage agroforestry for **dual climate mitigation and adaptation benefits**, including carbon sequestration and risk reduction (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/35592).
• **CGIAR (West/Central Africa CSA Alliances)** operationalized regional programs prioritizing **forest restoration and agroforestry for mitigation**, with co-benefits for adaptation in Central Africa (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/179555).
• **CGIAR (World Agroforestry Center)** analyzed **measurement, reporting, and verification (MRV) barriers** for agroforestry in UNFCCC processes, advocating for its inclusion in **Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions (NAMAs)** and REDD+ frameworks to unlock climate finance (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/98831, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/98829).
• **CGIAR (Forests & Climate Security)** examined conflicts and governance in forest-based mitigation initiatives, highlighting agroforestry’s role in **climate security and peacebuilding** in fragile states (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/116673).
• **CGIAR (Southern Africa)** quantified **spatial variation in aboveground carbon stocks** to inform agroforestry’s mitigation potential (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HN26SO).

wheat.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **wheat resilience and productivity** across **Adaptation, Water, and risk management** through **citizen science, crop modeling, and agronomic innovations**. Specifically:
- **Bioversity/CGIAR** advanced **decentralized breeding** in marginal environments (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OEZGVP).
- **ICARDA/CGIAR** deployed **real-time crop monitoring** via smartphone-based remote sensing to assess production risks (Source: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/DBAFZY).
- **CCAFS/CGIAR** piloted **climate-adaptive farming systems** (e.g., conservation agriculture) in India (Source: https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-09-2018-0065).
wheat.adaptation:
• **CGIAR (Bioversity/ICARDA)** developed **data-driven decentralized breeding** to increase genetic gain in marginal wheat-growing environments (e.g., Ethiopian highlands) by distributing genotype packages to 1,165 farmers for on-farm evaluation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/OEZGVP).

• **CGIAR (ICARDA/CCAFS)** implemented **citizen science trials (tricot method)** to evaluate climate-adaptive wheat varieties (e.g., 62 durum wheat and 21 bread wheat varieties) across Ethiopia and India, linking farmer observations to environmental data for yield and performance assessments (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4ICF6W).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS/ICARDA)** assessed **lockdown-related disruptions** in wheat production (e.g., crop losses, harvesting costs) via phone surveys with 1,767 Haryana farmers, documenting risk coping mechanisms and food security impacts during COVID-19 (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/PAWM4U).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS)** demonstrated **50% yield increases** in India through weather-resilient technologies (e.g., hailstorm/cold-wave mitigation) in districts like Betul and Nalanda (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109732).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS)** tested **bioeconomic adaptation strategies** (e.g., increased irrigation/fertilization) for hard wheat in Tunisia, finding partial compensation for climate-induced productivity declines (≤1°C warming) (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154919).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS)** deployed **real-time crop yield forecasting** in Nepal using the **CRAFT toolbox**, enabling pre-harvest estimates to inform food security planning amid climate uncertainty (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76383).

• **CGIAR (ICARDA)** promoted **conservation agriculture (CAW)** in Haryana as an *ex ante* climate risk strategy, showing adoption persistence post-extreme rainfall events (2014–2016) (https://doi.org/10.1108/ijccsm-09-2018-0065).
wheat.water:
• **CGIAR (ICARDA)** compiled a **standardized zinc agronomy database** for wheat in Ethiopia, analyzing Zn application methods (basal/foliar), rates, and soil covariates (e.g., DTPA-Zn, pH) to optimize nutrient use and yield (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WUZK95).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS/ICARDA)** highlighted **water availability as a critical constraint** for climate adaptation strategies (e.g., irrigation/fertilization) in Tunisia, where success depended on mobilizing additional water resources (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/154919).

• **CGIAR (CCAFS)** integrated **soil-water management** into real-time yield forecasting in Nepal, using meteorological and soil data to predict wheat productivity under variable precipitation (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/76383).

fisheries and aquaculture.narrative:
CGIAR’s research in **fisheries and aquaculture** advanced **resilience, productivity, and climate-informed innovation** across Africa and Asia. Specifically:
- **WorldFish** assessed **on-farm performance** of tilapia and catfish systems in **Nigeria and Egypt** to benchmark production efficiency (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9XLF4O, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/VSQWEE).
- The **Asia Africa BlueTech Superhighway (AABS)** and **PROFISHBLUE** projects scaled **digital tools** for fisheries management in **Kenya and Zambia**, integrating climate data and gender-inclusive adaptation strategies (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WOL3MR, https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J29HD).
fisheries and aquaculture.adaptation:
• **WorldFish** evaluated **gendered climate vulnerability** among fishers in **Zambia and Kenya**, linking adaptation strategies to livelihood resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/IQXBDI).
• The **Fish for Livelihoods project** in **Myanmar** mapped **farmers’ perceptions of climatic shocks** and adaptation practices (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZINQUU).
• **PROFISHBLUE** identified **climate risks in aquaculture/fisheries** in **Zambia and Malawi**, developing decision-support systems for weather-informed management (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/7J29HD).
• **AABS** assessed **digital tool adoption** for climate adaptation in **Kenya’s coastal fisheries**, focusing on weather forecasts and postharvest resilience (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/WOL3MR).
• **WorldFish** analyzed **tilapia farmers’ risk preferences** in **Myanmar**, linking productivity to climate adaptation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/D7FZLX).
fisheries and aquaculture.mitigation: • CGIAR’s **Aquatic Foods Initiative** researched **climate-sensitive disease dynamics** in aquaculture, proposing **adaptation/mitigation strategies** for virulent pathogen risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/66594).

livestock (general/small ruminants).narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced livestock and small ruminant systems through **climate-resilient innovations**, **adaptation strategies**, and **mitigation frameworks**, with a focus on smallholder integration, fodder systems, and sustainable land management. Specifically:
- **ILRI** and **CIAT** designed **climate-smart practices** for mixed crop-livestock systems, integrating **fodder trees, water conservation, and intercropping** to enhance resilience in Ethiopia and Ghana (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/H6RWOO, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92997).
- **ILRI** and **IFPRI** documented **household adaptation** to climate change, emphasizing **diversification into livestock** as a critical livelihood strategy in West Africa and Pakistan (Sources: https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/T9GGYA, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/12422).
- **CIAT** influenced **national mitigation policies** in Colombia by providing evidence for **low-emission dairy practices** and **adaptation plans** in water, biodiversity, and livestock sectors (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65221).
livestock (general/small ruminants).adaptation:
• **ILRI** introduced **sustainable intensification** in Ethiopia through **intercropping, water conservation practices, and integrated tree cropping** to address land degradation and fodder scarcity in mixed crop-livestock systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/H6RWOO).

• **ILRI/IFPRI** analyzed **climate vulnerabilities** in Zambia’s **groundnut, soybean, and milk value chains**, identifying **adaptation priorities** for Green Innovation Centres to manage climate risks (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/111415).

• **ILRI** documented **smallholder adaptation** in Burkina Faso and Ghana, where farmers integrated **livestock rearing** into agricultural practices to cope with drought and overgrazing, using **fodder trees** to improve dry-season feed availability (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/12422, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/92997).

• **ILRI/IFPRI** examined **mixed crop-livestock-tree (MCLT) systems** in Ghana, highlighting **climate-resilient innovation bundles** (e.g., **irrigation packages, circular farming**) to mitigate productivity declines and enhance food security (https://doi.org/10.5304/jafscd.2025.142.020).

• **CIAT** supported Colombia’s **National Planning Department** in developing **sector-level adaptation plans** for water, biodiversity, and livestock, based on CGIAR’s economic impact assessments of climate change (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65221).
livestock (general/small ruminants).mitigation:
• **CIAT** collaborated with Colombia’s **Ministry of Agriculture** to prioritize **mitigation actions** for the livestock sector, contributing to the **Colombian Low Emission Development Strategy (LEDS)** through evidence-based **Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions (NAMAs)** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/65221).

• **ILRI** scaled **climate-smart dairy practices** in Kenya, leveraging **mitigation benefits** from improved feed production and feeding practices to reduce greenhouse gas emissions while enhancing resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/67901).
livestock (general/small ruminants).water:
• **ILRI/Bioversity** evaluated **genotypic responses** of *Canavalia brasiliensis* (a tropical forage legume) to **water stress and soil pH**, measuring physiological and morphological traits to inform drought-resistant livestock feed systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/HWLPUK).

• **ILRI/IFPRI** integrated **water conservation practices** into Ethiopia’s **Africa RISING program**, linking them to **sustainable intensification** and **intercropping** for mixed crop-livestock systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/H6RWOO).

• **ILRI** assessed **agricultural water use** in Pakistan’s rural households, linking water management to **livestock productivity** and climate change adaptation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/T9GGYA).

• **ILRI** analyzed **land-use/cover changes** in Ethiopia, quantifying **cropland/grazing land dynamics** to address **feed resource competition** and land degradation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/8IXPXX).

livestock (dairy).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient and low-emission dairy systems** across Asia and Africa through **adaptation strategies** (e.g., farmer-led networks, improved forages) and **mitigation interventions** (e.g., GHG reduction guidelines, carbon footprint assessments). Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)** designed **greenhouse gas mitigation guidelines** for dairy farmers in China, focusing on feed and manure management (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119591**).
- **International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)** advanced **Climate-Smart Dairy (CSD) systems** in East Africa via socio-economic surveys and improved feeding strategies (Source: **https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9GBKPW**).
livestock (dairy).adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** conducted a **socio-economic baseline survey** for Climate-Smart Dairy systems in East Africa, analyzing livestock feeding strategies, resilience, and household adaptation (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/9GBKPW).

• **ILRI** facilitated **farmer-led adaptation networks** in Kenya, promoting diverse crop-livestock systems and improved feeding practices to enhance milk yields and climate resilience (Sources: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/175675**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/175700**).

• **ILRI and CCAFS** integrated **gender-inclusive extension services** and fodder supply into Kenya’s Dairy NAMA to boost on-farm productivity (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121866**).

• **ILRI** assessed **climate change impacts on forage grasses** in Ethiopia, projecting suitability shifts for Buffel, Rhodes, and Napier grasses under future climates (Source: **https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-23461-w**).
livestock (dairy).mitigation:
• **CGIAR’s CCAFS** developed **greenhouse gas mitigation guidelines** for dairy farmers in China, covering feed management, manure management, and total-farm approaches (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/119591**).

• **ILRI and partners** outlined **GHG mitigation strategies** for Kenyan dairy systems, including biogas digesters, reduced milk waste, and energy efficiency (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/175143**).

• **Kenya’s State Department of Livestock (supported by CGIAR/CCAFS)** catalyzed **USD 223 million in investments** for a **Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Action (NAMA)** in dairy, aligned with the Green Climate Fund (Source: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/106306**).

• **CGIAR researchers** presented **carbon footprint assessments** for Chinese dairy systems, identifying mitigation options (Sources: **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97868**, **https://hdl.handle.net/10568/97867**).

dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **climate-resilient dryland cereals (sorghum/millets)** through **adaptation-focused research** across Africa and Asia, addressing food security, yield stagnation, and climate vulnerability. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s Climate Change and Food Security Program** conducted **climate risk assessments** for sorghum in Tanzania and Kenya, identifying stagnated yields due to lack of commercial markets and low agricultural potential (Sources: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107719, https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107722).
- **ICRISAT and CGIAR’s Dryland Cereals Program** developed **weather-resilient technologies** for pearl millet in India, addressing lodging risks from excess rainfall and poor irrigation (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109724).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).adaptation:
• **CGIAR’s Climate Risk Assessment Team** analyzed stagnated sorghum yields in Tanzania despite expanded cultivation, attributing the trend to **lack of commercial markets and limited access to improved varieties** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107719).

• **CGIAR-ICRISAT** mapped sorghum’s **agro-climatic thresholds** (250 mm rainfall, 10°C minimum temperature) in Kenya, highlighting its role as a staple in low-potential regions (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/107722).

• **CGIAR’s Crop Simulation Modeling Group** evaluated **fertility scenarios** (organic/inorganic) for sorghum in Mali, integrating **CMIP5 climate projections** to assess adaptation cost-benefits under soil moisture constraints (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SGHJ3J).

• **CGIAR’s Resilient Crops Program** promoted **weather-resilient pearl millet practices** in India’s Mathura district, mitigating lodging risks from excess rainfall (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/109724).

• **CGIAR’s Physiology Research** investigated **delayed flowering in sorghum** under winter sowing, attributing prostrate growth to **UV radiation cues** (not night temperatures) in Mali (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/23780).

• **CGIAR-APSIM Modeling Team** tested **adaptation strategies** (cultivar type, planting windows, fertilizer levels) for pearl millet in Nigeria/Senegal, showing **dual-purpose varieties and early sowing** reduced climate-change yield losses by up to 8% (https://doi.org/10.3389/fagro.2024.1428630).

• **CGIAR’s InfoCrop-SORGHUM Model** projected **14–32% yield losses** in India by 2080 due to climate change, but demonstrated **adaptation strategies** (variety shifts, sowing dates) could reduce vulnerability by 10% in monsoon sorghum (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agee.2010.04.012).

• **CGIAR’s APSIM Modeling** in West Africa showed **early-maturing sorghum varieties** gained **11–16% yield** under RCP 8.5 scenarios, validating **plant trait adjustments** (RUE, phyllochron) for resilience (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fcr.2019.107685).

• **CGIAR-ICRISAT’s DSSAT Simulations** emphasized **location-specific breeding** for drought-tolerant pearl millet in India/West Africa, using climate projections to guide cultivar selection (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21421/D2/XDNUE8).

• **CGIAR’s Heat Stress Analysis** mapped **probability of >31–45°C during Rabi sorghum flowering** in India, identifying critical heat tolerance thresholds (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.21421/D2/LJQX96).
dryland cereals (sorghum/millets).water: • **CGIAR’s Crop Simulation Models** integrated **soil moisture availability** with climate projections to evaluate **fertility adaptation options** for sorghum in Mali’s Sudanian region (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/SGHJ3J).

forages and pastures.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced **forages and pastures** through **germplasm evaluation, agronomic trait selection, and climate-resilient production systems** across Latin America and Ethiopia. Specifically:
- **CIAT** evaluated **adaptation potential of tropical pasture germplasm** in semievergreen forests via the RIEPT network (Source A).
- **CIAT/CORPOICA** identified **superior Brachiaria accessions** with climate-adaptive traits through multilocational trials (Source B).
- **CIAT** analyzed **genotype-environment interactions** in *Desmodium ovalifolium* to mitigate tannin variability under varying soil/climate conditions (Source C).
- **ICARDA/ILRI** assessed **land suitability and supplemental irrigation needs** for alfalfa under climate change in Ethiopia (Source D).
forages and pastures.adaptation:
• **CIAT** established the **RIEPT network** to evaluate pasture germplasm adaptation across tropical ecosystems, comparing productivity, persistence, and management efficiency under grazing systems (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/ZB4DYK).
• **CIAT/CORPOICA** selected **Brachiaria accessions** with superior agronomic traits, testing climate-soil interactions on yield across 11 Colombian sites (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NGL4OG).
• **CIAT** conducted **multilocational trials** on *Desmodium ovalifolium* to quantify soil fertility and climate effects on tannin content, addressing anti-quality factors (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/COQEK8).
forages and pastures.water: • **ICARDA/ILRI** developed a **simplified land-suitability framework** for alfalfa production in Ethiopia, integrating supplemental irrigation needs and climate change impact assessments (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123185).

bananas.narrative:
CGIAR’s research centers advanced banana resilience and productivity through targeted genetic and data-driven innovations. Specifically:
- **Bioversity International** identified water-efficient banana genotypes via high-precision hydroponic phenotyping (Source A).
- **CGIAR’s disease resistance program** mapped differentially expressed genes in Fusarium-resistant and -susceptible banana varieties to inform breeding strategies (Source B).
- **CGIAR’s data science initiatives** empowered Colombian banana exporters with climate-smart decision tools via advanced data mining (Source C).
bananas.adaptation:
• CGIAR’s **disease resistance research** conducted transcriptomic analysis to identify differentially expressed genes (DEGs) in banana corms resistant to *Fusarium oxysporum* tropical race 4 (TR4), comparing *Musa acuminata* ‘Pahang’ (resistant) and Cavendish ‘Brazilian’ (susceptible) varieties, with functional enrichment via GO/KEGG pathways (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/W2M6WG).
• CGIAR facilitated **climate-smart decision-making** in Colombia’s banana sector by enabling farmers’ organizations (e.g., Grupo Santa Maria) to adopt data-driven methods for improved resilience (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/121261).
bananas.water: • CGIAR’s **Bioversity International** developed lab-based hydroponic phenotyping to quantify water loss patterns and transpiration in banana genotypes, selecting for water-efficient traits under controlled LED conditions (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/NHVBN5).

livestock (small).narrative:
CGIAR advanced **small livestock research** in **Ethiopia and regional smallholder systems** through **methodological innovation and adaptive feeding strategies**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR’s livestock researchers** refined **Tier 1 and Tier 2 methodologies** for estimating enteric methane emissions in smallholder sheep systems (Source A).
- **CGIAR’s adaptation team** documented **homemade concentrate preparation techniques** to optimize sheep fattening efficiency in Basona Werena and neighboring districts (Source B).
livestock (small).adaptation: • CGIAR’s **livestock adaptation research team** demonstrated **homemade concentrate preparation methods**—rich in carbohydrates, protein, and minerals—to accelerate sheep fattening (70–90 days) in Basona Werena and adjacent districts, replacing factory by-product feeds (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/168846).
livestock (small).mitigation: • CGIAR **developed seasonal enteric methane emission factors** for smallholder sheep in Ethiopia by comparing **CSIRO and IPCC Tier 2 methodologies**, integrating live weight data, feed quality, and digestibility to estimate **daily methane production (DMP)** and annual emission factors (https://dataverse.harvard.edu/dataset.xhtml?persistentId=doi:10.7910/DVN/4K20I6).

fruits and vegetables.narrative:
CGIAR supported **climate-resilient agricultural systems in Burkina Faso** through **water access and crop diversification**. Specifically:
- **CGIAR researchers facilitated microfinance-enabled small-scale irrigation** (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061).
- **The Center promoted vegetable-based crop diversification** to sustain livelihoods during dry seasons (Source: https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061).
fruits and vegetables.adaptation: • CGIAR **enabled crop diversification (vegetables) in Burkina Faso** to extend productivity into dry seasons, improving food security and income for smallholder farmers (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061).
fruits and vegetables.water: • CGIAR **supported smallholder access to water** via microfinance-backed pumps, drawing from nearby dams to sustain vegetable production (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/75061).

general cereals.narrative:
CGIAR prioritized **sustainable intensification of irrigated cereal systems** in **sub-tropical and semi-arid climates** through **precision agronomic innovations**. Specifically:
- CGIAR re-designed **irrigated intensive cereal systems** by bundling precision agronomic innovations to enhance water-use efficiency and sustainability in **North-West India** (https://hdl.handle.net/10568/123017).
